The KJV 'slime' for *chemar* is archaic (16th-century English for any sticky tar-like substance); 'bitumen' is the accurate modern term — the natural asphaltic seepage of Mesopotamia (especially the Hit region on the middle Euphrates), which was used as construction mortar from the Ubaid period (5th millennium BCE) onward. The chapter is technically literate about the world it describes — the WoH translation preserves the technical accuracy.

terms.literal: and a tower, and its head in the skies
terms.standardEnglish: and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven
terms.wohChoice: a tower with its top in the skies
terms.rationale: *Migdal v'rosho bashamayim* — *a tower with its top in the skies*. *Migdal* (מִגְדָּל) is the Hebrew word for a tower or fortified high structure (city walls, watchtowers, siege towers in 2 Sam 22:51, Isa 33:18). In Gen 11:4 the *migdal* names the structure the Mesopotamian context strongly indicates: a ziggurat — the stepped temple-platform of Mesopotamian religious architecture, exemplified by the great ziggurat of Etemenanki at Babylon. // The phrase *rosho bashamayim* (literally 'its head in the skies') is a stock-phrase for *very tall* (cf. Deut 1:28, 9:1, of fortified Canaanite cities 'whose walls reach up to the skies'). But in this chapter the phrase functions on two levels: as hyperbole for height, *and* as a description of the ziggurat's theological function — the structure was designed in Mesopotamian theology to be the stairway between earth and the divine realm, with the deity descending onto the platform-shrine at the summit and human worship ascending through it. *Rosho bashamayim* therefore names both the physical aspiration and the cosmological function. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'top in the skies' (using *skies* from the established *shamayim* glossary entry); the standard English 'whose top may reach to heaven' (KJV/ASV) smooths the construction and obscures the ziggurat-specific reference. Reclassified as `inferred` because the strong reading of *rosho bashamayim* as ziggurat-function-marker (rather than mere hyperbole) is interpretive — defensible from ANE archaeological context and supported by scholars (Speiser, Westermann), but not consensus.

terms.literal: and let us make for ourselves a name
terms.standardEnglish: let us make us a name
terms.wohChoice: let us make for ourselves a name
terms.rationale: *Naase lanu shem* — *let us make for ourselves a name* — uses the **same cohortative form** as the divine *naase adam* of Gen 1:26. *Naase* is first-person plural cohortative of עָשָׂה *asah* ('make, do'). In Genesis 1:26 the speaker is the Elohim, the cohortative is interpreted as divine plural; here in 11:4 the speakers are humans, the cohortative is straightforwardly first-person plural. The verbal parallel is exact and structurally important: the chapter is staging humans assuming the *naase* register that opened the creation account. // *Shem* (שֵׁם) in Hebrew Bible idiom is *name* in the sense of *renown, reputation, lasting memory* (cf. 2 Sam 7:9, of YHWH making David's *shem* great; Isa 56:5, of an *everlasting name*). The phrase *anshei ha-shem* — *men of the name* — was applied in Gen 6:4 to the antediluvian *gibborim* (the offspring of the *bnei ha-elohim*). The Babel-builders are now collectively saying: *let us make for ourselves a name*. The chapter is verbally connecting the human ambition at Babel to the *gibborim*-class register the Flood was supposed to have ended. The translation preserves the bare cohortative without editorializing; the commentary surfaces the parallel.

terms.literal: lest we be scattered upon the face of all the land
terms.standardEnglish: lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth
terms.wohChoice: lest we be scattered upon the face of the whole land
terms.rationale: The Babel-builders' motive-clause: *pen-naputz al-pnei kol ha-aretz* — *lest we be scattered upon the face of the whole land*. The conjunction פֶּן *pen* introduces a negative purpose-clause ('lest, in order that not'). The verb *naputz* is qal passive of פּוּץ *putz* 'to scatter, disperse'. The same root will return in v8 (*vayyafetz YHWH otam ... al-pnei kol ha-aretz* — 'YHWH scattered them ... upon the face of the whole land') and v9 (the closing summary). The chapter's structural irony is built on this root: what the Babel-builders fear in v4 is exactly what YHWH does in v8 — the *same verb*, the *same prepositional phrase*. The WoH translation preserves the verbal repetition (translating *putz* consistently as 'scatter' in both clauses) so the chapter's reversal-structure is visible. The standard English 'scattered abroad' (KJV) adds an 'abroad' the Hebrew does not have.

terms.literal: and YHWH came down to see
terms.standardEnglish: And the LORD came down to see
terms.rationale: *Vayered YHWH lir'ot* — *YHWH came down to see*. The verb is יָרַד *yarad* 'descend', used with YHWH as subject. The anthropomorphic register is plain: YHWH is positioned *above*, the city-and-tower is *below*, and YHWH *descends* to inspect. The descent-language sets up the *havah neredah* (let us also go down) of v7, where the divine plural cohortative will act. // The verb *yarad* is used elsewhere of divine descent at Sinai (Ex 19:11, 19:18, 19:20, 'YHWH came down on Mount Sinai'); the verbal echo across the Babel and Sinai narratives is one of the Pentateuch's longer-range structural moves. The translation preserves the bare anthropomorphic register without softening to 'looked down' or 'observed from above'.

terms.source: וְעַתָּה לֹא־יִבָּצֵר מֵהֶם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יָזְמוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת
terms.translit: v'atah lo yibatzer mehem kol asher yazmu la'asot
terms.literal: and now nothing will be made inaccessible from them, all that they have planned to do
terms.standardEnglish: and now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do
terms.wohChoice: and now, nothing will be withheld from them which they have schemed to do
terms.rationale: *Lo yibatzer mehem kol asher yazmu la'asot* — the Hebrew is dense. *Yibatzer* is niphal of בָּצַר *batsar*, whose semantic range covers 'restrain, cut off, make inaccessible, be impossible' (Job 42:2 uses the same passive of YHWH: *lo yibatzer mimkha mezimah* — 'no purpose of yours is restrained'). The negation *lo yibatzer* means *will not be withheld / made inaccessible*. *Yazmu* is qal perfect of זָמַם *zamam* 'to devise, scheme, plan' — neutral-to-negative in semantic range, often used of *premeditation* in legal contexts. // YHWH's assessment is plain: human coordination at this scale produces unlimited capacity. The clause is *not* judicial-curse language; it is *engineering-assessment* language. The response in v7 will be a targeted intervention to break the coordination — not punishment but a cap on capacity. The Wheel of Heaven lens reads this verse with attention: the chapter is staging a strategic divine response to human power-aggregation, and the verb *yibatzer* names the divine concern precisely. The WoH translation 'nothing will be withheld' preserves the passive-voice claim; 'schemed' renders *zamam* in its slightly-negative tonal range without over-determining (KJV 'imagined' reads as merely cognitive, missing the deliberate-planning sense). Reclassified `inferred` because the rendering of *zamam* as 'schemed' (vs. 'planned' or 'imagined') is a connotational choice.

terms.literal: come! let us go down and let us confuse there their lip
terms.standardEnglish: Go to, let us go down, and there confound their language
terms.wohChoice: Come, let us go down and confuse their tongue there
terms.rationale: *Havah neredah v'navlah* — *come, let us go down, and let us confuse*. This is the **third explicit divine cohortative in Genesis**, after 1:26 (*naase adam* — let us make humankind) and 3:22 (*hen ha-adam haya k'achad mimennu* — behold, the human has become like one of us). All three are first-person plural divine speech. The chapter's cohortative is the structural mirror of the human cohortatives in vv 3-4 (*havah nilbenah*, *havah nivneh*) — the divine response to the human project uses the same rhetorical form. // *Neredah* is first-person plural cohortative of יָרַד *yarad* 'descend'. The verb mirrors the singular *vayered* of v5 ('YHWH came down to see'); v7's plural neredah indicates either a divine-council descent or a self-summoning rhetorical plural (the latter is the standard non-WoH reading; the former is the modern divine-council scholarship reading favored by Heiser and others, and is consistent with the WoH lens). // *Navlah* is first-person plural cohortative of בָּלַל *balal* 'to mix, confound, confuse' — the etymological root of *Babel*. The intervention is linguistic: not destroying the city, not killing the builders, but breaking the coordination-capability by fragmenting the shared language. The WoH translation preserves the cohortative structure ('let us go down ... let us confuse') and the linguistic-targeting register.

terms.literal: and YHWH scattered them from there
terms.standardEnglish: So the LORD scattered them abroad from thence
terms.rationale: *Vayyafetz YHWH otam misham* — *YHWH scattered them from there*. The verb is hiphil (causative) of פּוּץ *putz* — the same root that the Babel-builders had used in v4 (*pen-naputz* — 'lest we be scattered'). The Hebrew is doing structural irony with verbal precision: the humans feared *being scattered* (passive); YHWH *scatters* them (active causative). The chapter's reversal is built into the same triconsonantal root, recurring four times across vv 4-9. The translation preserves the verbal repetition ('scatter' for *putz* in both passive and causative) so the symmetry is visible.

terms.source: עַל־כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ בָּבֶל כִּי־שָׁם בָּלַל יְהוָה שְׂפַת כָּל־הָאָרֶץ
terms.translit: al-ken qara shemah Bavel ki sham balal YHWH sefat kol ha-aretz
terms.literal: therefore its name was called Babel, because there YHWH confounded the lip of all the land
terms.standardEnglish: Therefore is the name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth
terms.wohChoice: Therefore its name was called Babel, because there YHWH confounded the tongue of the whole land
terms.rationale: *Bavel* (בָּבֶל) and *balal* (בָּלַל) share a triconsonantal root structure close enough for the chapter to assert an etymological pun. The actual etymology is not internal to Hebrew: *Bavel* most likely derives from Akkadian *Bab-ilim* (*'gate of the gods'* or *'gate of god'* — *bab-ili* in the singular) — the same root that gives English 'Babylon' via Greek Βαβυλών. The chapter is offering a folk etymology that *recodes* the Mesopotamian self-naming ('gate of the gods') as the Hebrew theological re-naming ('the place where YHWH confounded'). The pun is theological recoding: the city of divine access is recast as the city of divine refusal of access. The translation preserves the bare etymology-clause and notes the recoding in commentary. *Bavel* (not Babylon, not Babel-in-English) is the Hebrew form; the English-tradition 'Babel' is acceptable as it tracks the Hebrew consonantal skeleton closely.

terms.translit: vayyoled et Avram et Nachor v'et Haran
terms.literal: and fathered Abram, Nachor, and Haran
terms.standardEnglish: and begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran
terms.rationale: Terah's three sons are named with the standard Hebrew transliterations: *Avram* (אַבְרָם — later renamed Avraham/Abraham in Gen 17), *Nachor* (נָחוֹר — same name as Terah's father in v24, the grandson named for the grandfather), *Haran* (הָרָן — distinct from the city *Charan* of v31, which has a different initial consonant in Hebrew: ח vs ה). // The naming triad establishes the patriarchal-family network: Abram will be the covenant-line; Nachor will remain in northern Mesopotamia and become the ancestor of Rebekah (Gen 24); Haran will die early but father Lot, Milkah, and (per 11:29) Yiskah. The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew forms ('Avram' not 'Abram', 'Nachor' not 'Nahor', 'Haran' not 'Haran-with-h'). However, the chapter retains 'Abram' (with English-traditional initial A and -br-) to match standard scholarly conventions and the canon-tradition of the name; full transliteration to 'Avram' is deferred to a future glossary decision.

*Ur Kasdim* — Ur of the Kasdim — names the family's place of origin. Ur was the great Sumerian city of southern Mesopotamia, capital of the Third Dynasty of Ur (ca. 2100 BCE), excavated by Sir Leonard Woolley in the 1920s. The qualifier *Kasdim* (Chaldeans) is **chronologically anachronistic**: the Chaldeans (Akkadian *Kaldu*) are a first-millennium-BCE Aramean people who came to dominate Babylonia in the Neo-Babylonian period (the most famous Chaldean being Nebuchadnezzar II). The phrase *Ur Kasdim* therefore mixes a third-millennium city with a first-millennium people's name — an editorial gloss reflecting the period in which the genealogy was redacted. // Modern scholarship has proposed alternative identifications (a northern Mesopotamian *Ur* near Haran, rather than the famous southern *Ur*) precisely to avoid the *Kasdim* anachronism. The southern-Ur identification remains dominant. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew *Kasdim* rather than the English-traditional 'Chaldees' (KJV) or 'Chaldeans' (modern translations) — both of which obscure the chronological seam by translating to a different language's name for the same people.

terms.literal: Sarai ... Milkah daughter of Haran ... Yiskah
terms.standardEnglish: Sarai ... Milcah, the daughter of Haran ... Iscah
terms.wohChoice: Sarai ... Milkah, daughter of Haran ... Yiskah
terms.rationale: Three women are named in Gen 11:29. *Sarai* (שָׂרַי, 'my princess' or 'noble lady', later renamed Sarah in 17:15) is Abram's wife; her lineage is *not* given (the narrative will eventually identify her in 20:12 as Abram's half-sister, with the same father but different mother). *Milkah* (מִלְכָּה, 'queen') is Nachor's wife, identified as Haran's daughter — making her Nachor's niece (intra-family marriage was the patriarchal-period norm). *Yiskah* (יִסְכָּה, etymology uncertain — possibly 'she sees' or 'anointed') is named only here in the Hebrew Bible; she is identified as another daughter of Haran but never reappears. // Rabbinic tradition (Megillah 14a, Sanhedrin 69b) identifies Yiskah with Sarai, on the basis that Sarai's lineage is otherwise unexplained and Yiskah is named without further mention. This identification has no textual support and is rejected by most modern scholarship (Sarai's later identification in 20:12 as Abram's half-sister, with Terah as her father, does not match Yiskah being Haran's daughter). The translation preserves the Hebrew forms and lets the textual crux stand.

terms.source: וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד
terms.translit: vat'hi Sarai aqarah ein lah valad
terms.literal: and was Sarai barren — no to her child
terms.standardEnglish: But Sarai was barren; she had no child
terms.wohChoice: Sarai was barren — she had no child
terms.rationale: Six Hebrew words name the constraint that will be load-bearing for the next ten chapters. *Aqarah* (עֲקָרָה) is the Hebrew word for 'barren', applied in the Hebrew Bible to Sarah (Gen 11:30, 16:1, 18:11-12), Rebekah (25:21), Rachel (29:31), Hannah (1 Sam 1:2), Manoah's wife (Judg 13:2), and others — a recurring female-barrenness motif tied to divine intervention and late-life conception. The redundant *ein lah valad* — *she had no child* — drives the assertion home: this is not delayed conception but stated barrenness as a baseline. // The verse is the chapter's pivot from primaeval-history scope to patriarchal-narrative scope: the constraint that the next ten chapters will work to overcome is named in a single short verse, without commentary. The chapter's textual economy at this point is structurally important — the next chapter (Gen 12) opens with divine action that will need to overcome what 11:30 has just stated as a flat impossibility.

terms.source: וַיֵּצְאוּ אִתָּם מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד־חָרָן וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם
terms.translit: vayyetz'u itam me-Ur Kasdim lalekhet artzah Kena'an vayyavo'u ad-Charan vayyeshvu sham
terms.literal: and they went out with them from Ur of the Kasdim to go toward the land of Canaan, and they came as far as Haran and settled there
terms.standardEnglish: and they went forth with them from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there
terms.wohChoice: they went out with them from Ur of the Kasdim to go to the land of Canaan, and they came to Haran and settled there
terms.rationale: The verse stages the *unfinished* migration toward Canaan. *Vayyetz'u me-Ur Kasdim* — 'they went out from Ur Kasdim' — names the departure; *lalekhet artzah Kena'an* — 'to go toward the land of Canaan' — names the intent; *vayyavo'u ad-Charan vayyeshvu sham* — 'and they came as far as Haran and settled there' — names the interruption. *Ad* (עַד) — 'as far as, up to' — marks Haran as a stopping-point short of the destination, not the destination itself. // *Charan* (חָרָן, with initial *chet*) is distinct from *Haran* (הָרָן, with initial *he*, the brother of Abram). The two are sometimes confused in English transliteration where both lose the diacritic distinction; in Hebrew they are different consonants and entirely different names. *Charan* is a major northern Mesopotamian city (the site near modern Harran in southeastern Turkey), an important Bronze and Iron Age trading center on the Aramean / Mesopotamian frontier and one of the main centers of the Sin (moon-god) cult. // Terah does not complete the migration; he dies in Charan (v32). Chapter 12 will open with YHWH's call to Abram to *go from your land, from your kindred, from your father's house, to the land that I will show you* (12:1) — and Abram will complete the migration his father had begun and abandoned. The chapter is staging the seam: the family-line journey toward Canaan stalls at Charan; the covenant-line journey will resume under explicit divine call.

The phrase opens Ezekiel 1:1. *Mar'ot* (מַרְאוֹת) is the plural of *mar'eh* (vision, appearance); the plural form is meaningful — Ezekiel saw multiple visions, not a single image. The genitive *Elohim* preserves the plural class-noun rendering established throughout the WoH translation (see [`elohim-as-translation`](/wiki/elohim/)). The phrase recurs in Ezek 8:3 and 40:2, opening the book's major vision-sequences. The standard English 'visions of God' singularizes both nouns and obscures the plural register; the WoH translation preserves both plurals.

The Kebar (Hebrew כְּבָר, Akkadian *naru kabari* — 'the great canal') is a major canal of the Babylonian canal system, branching from the Euphrates near Nippur in southern Mesopotamia. The location is well-attested in cuneiform sources from the Neo-Babylonian period. Ezekiel's vision happens by this canal, in the Jewish exilic community settled in southern Babylonia. The KJV transliteration 'Chebar' is etymologically defensible but reads as the English-tradition rendering; the WoH translation preserves the Hebrew form 'Kebar' (also matching modern scholarly conventions and the Akkadian original closer than 'Chebar'). The phrase recurs at Ezek 1:3, 3:15, 10:15, 10:20, 10:22, 43:3 as the book's geographic anchor.

Yoyakhin (יוֹיָכִין, also spelled Yehoyakhin / יְהוֹיָכִין in full form) was the penultimate king of Judah, deported to Babylon in 597 BCE in the first Babylonian deportation (2 Kgs 24:8-17). The 'fifth year of his exile' = 593 BCE, the date of Ezekiel's vision. Modern English Bibles render the name as 'Jehoiachin' (KJV/ASV) — an Anglicization of the longer Hebrew form via the Vulgate. The WoH translation uses the shorter Hebrew form Yoyakhin, closer to the consonantal text and aligning with the WoH project's general preference for Hebrew-form proper names (Yapheth, Yoqtan, Yiskah). Yoyakhin is also the form used in the Babylonian ration-tablets ('Yaukin') that confirm the historicity of his exile.

*Eretz Kasdim* — 'land of the Kasdim' — names Babylonia. The Kasdim (Akkadian *Kaldu*, English 'Chaldeans') are a first-millennium-BCE Aramean people who came to dominate Babylonia under the Neo-Babylonian dynasty (Nabopolassar, Nebuchadnezzar II). The phrase recurs at Ezek 1:3 and is the book's term for the exile-location. Parallels `ur-kasdim` (Gen 11:28, 31) — both use the same Aramean-period naming. The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew form *Kasdim* rather than the English-traditional 'Chaldeans' for consistency with the existing `ur-kasdim` entry.

*Yad YHWH* — 'the hand of YHWH' — is a technical Hebrew Bible idiom for divine agency or compulsion. In Ezekiel specifically, the phrase is a recurring formula marking the onset of prophetic experience: *vat'hi alav yad YHWH* — 'the hand of YHWH was upon him' — at 1:3, 3:14, 3:22, 8:1, 33:22, 37:1, 40:1. The phrase recurs in Elijah-Elisha narratives (1 Kgs 18:46, 2 Kgs 3:15) and in Isa 8:11 for similar prophetic-impulse moments. The lexical core is *yad* (hand), anthropomorphically applied to YHWH but covering the broader sense of *power, agency, custody, compulsion*. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'hand of YHWH' and the YHWH name (not 'the LORD'). The Christian-tradition 'the LORD' is a Vulgate-via-LXX substitution-tradition; the Hebrew uses the divine name.

terms.literal: Yehezkel son of Buzi the priest
terms.standardEnglish: Ezekiel the son of Buzi the priest
terms.wohChoice: Ezekiel son of Buzi the priest
terms.rationale: Ezekiel (יְחֶזְקֵאל Yehezkel — 'El strengthens' or 'may El strengthen') is identified by patronymic (*ben Buzi*) and office (*ha-kohen* — the priest). The priestly identity is structurally important: Ezekiel is a *kohen*, an Aaronic-lineage priest, displaced from the Jerusalem Temple by the 597 BCE deportation. His prophetic-vision register throughout the book is recognizably *priestly* — spatially calibrated, ritually inflected, concerned with the temple's defilement and restoration — in a way that contrasts with the prophetic-rhetorical register of his contemporary Jeremiah (in Jerusalem). The WoH translation preserves 'Ezekiel' (the standard English form) for the prophet's name, balancing the WoH preference for Hebrew-form names against the strongly-established English-tradition rendering of this specific name (the book title 'Ezekiel' is universal in English). The book's catalog title across all WoH metadata uses 'Ezekiel'. *Buzi* and *kohen* render in their Hebrew transliteration / structural form.

*Ruach se'arah* — 'a stormy wind' — opens Ezekiel's vision (1:4) as the carrier of the approaching apparition. *Ruach* is the standard Hebrew word for *wind / breath / spirit* (see `ruach-elohim` for the Gen 1:2 cosmogonic sense); *se'arah* is *storm, tempest* (specifically, a violent or whirling wind). The compound phrase recurs in 2 Kgs 2:11 (the *se'arah* that took Elijah up) and in prophetic-judgment texts (Jer 23:19, 30:23). The Ezekiel verse uses the phrase as the *transport-medium* of the chayyot apparatus — the chayyot come from the north *in* (or *with*) a stormy wind. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'stormy wind' rather than KJV/ASV 'whirlwind' (which over-specifies the rotation register).

The Hebrew word *chashmal* (חַשְׁמַל) appears in the Hebrew Bible **only three times** (Ezek 1:4, 1:27, 8:2) and has no clear Hebrew etymology. The word is one of the most contested in the entire Hebrew Bible. // The LXX (3rd-2nd c. BCE Greek translation) renders ἤλεκτρον (*elektron*) — Greek for the gold-silver alloy *electrum*, used for cultic vessels and royal regalia in classical antiquity. The Vulgate (Latin) follows: *electrum*. KJV and ASV render 'amber' (a different substance — fossilized tree resin — but also associated with golden-yellow luminous color); NJPS renders 'electrum'; NRSV 'gleaming amber'; ESV 'gleaming metal'. The modern scholarly consensus favors 'electrum' or 'gleaming metal'. // Akkadian *elmešu* (a precious gleaming substance used in royal-religious contexts, possibly amber or amber-glass) has been proposed as a cognate. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew word *chashmal* untranslated — parallel to how *Elohim* and *YHWH* are preserved. The reader encounters the chapter's own technical vocabulary on the page. The commentary supplies the lexical context; the translation does not pretend to know what *chashmal* IS. // Modern Hebrew uses *chashmal* for *electricity* (a 19th-century coinage by Eliezer Ben-Yehuda, who reused the biblical word for the new concept) — itself an interesting datum: the biblical term was the natural Hebrew word for a luminous-electrical phenomenon. Reclassified as `inferred` because the preservation-of-Hebrew choice (vs. translation to a specific English equivalent) is a translator's interpretive call.

*Chayyot* (חַיּוֹת) is the plural of *chayyah* — *living thing, animal, life-form*. The root is *chai* (alive, life), the same root behind *chayyim* (life-plural, often used as a singular abstract). The chapter uses *chayyot* as a technical class-noun for the four figures (1:5, 13-15, 19-22), not as the generic biological term (cattle, wild animal). Apocalyptic literature picks up the term: Revelation 4:6's four *zoa* (Greek 'living things') is a direct re-reading of Ezekiel's chayyot. The English convention 'living creatures' is well-established and the WoH translation adopts it; 'living beings' would also be defensible (especially given that the chapter goes to such lengths to describe the figures' un-creature-like attributes). The translation preserves the *chai*-root register that links these figures to the broader Hebrew Bible vocabulary of *life*.

terms.standardEnglish: appearance of likeness / the likeness as the appearance of
terms.rationale: Ezekiel 1 deploys a distinctive hedging-formula throughout: *mar'eh* (appearance) + *demut* (likeness), with various combinations of *ke-ein* ('like the look of'), *kemar'eh* ('like the appearance of'), and *demut* ('likeness of'). The chapter is methodically cautious — Ezekiel reports what things *looked like* and what they had the *likeness of*, not what they were. The formula reaches its highest density at 1:26 (*demut k'mar'eh adam* — 'likeness of an appearance like human') and 1:28 (*hu mar'eh demut kvod YHWH* — 'this was the appearance of the likeness of the glory of YHWH'). // The WoH translation preserves the doubled hedging formula consistently, surfacing the chapter's epistemic discipline. The reader sees Ezekiel describing-by-likeness, not asserting-identity. This is a translator's gift to the reader: the chapter teaches its own reading-protocol — *here is what it looked like*, not *here is what it was*.

The numeric signature *four* is the chapter's structural backbone: four chayyot, four faces per chayyah (human, lion, ox, eagle — v10), four wings per chayyah (v6, with the two-up-two-down configuration described in v11), four wheels (v15), four directions of motion (v9, v12, v17, v20). Four is the standard Hebrew Bible number for geographic-directional completeness (four winds, four corners of the earth, four rivers of Eden). The chayyot's design is calibrated to omnidirectional simultaneous-motion: each face oriented in one of the four cardinal directions, allowing the system to move any direction without rotation (v9, v12, v17). The composite face design — human, lion, ox, eagle — represents the four 'rulers' of ANE biological hierarchy: human (intellect), lion (wild fauna), ox (domesticated fauna), eagle (avifauna). The translation preserves the literal numerics; commentary surfaces the structural signature.

terms.standardEnglish: a wheel in the middle of a wheel
terms.wohChoice: as if a wheel were within a wheel
terms.rationale: The chapter's most-famous phrase outside of *chashmal*. The Hebrew is *ka-asher yihyeh ha-ofan b'tokh ha-ofan* — *as if the wheel were inside the wheel*. The construction uses Ezekiel's signature hedging formula (*ka-asher yihyeh* — 'as if it would be'); he is reporting what the wheels *looked like*, not asserting that wheels were literally inside wheels. // The standard scholarly reading: two wheels intersecting at right angles (gimbal-like / orthogonal-axis configuration), allowing the wheel-system to rotate in any direction without turning the chayyot above it. This matches the chapter's running concern with omnidirectional motion (v9, v12, v17, v21). // *Ofan* (אוֹפַן) is the standard Hebrew noun for *wheel* — chariot-wheel, mill-wheel, any rotating disc. *Ofannim* is the plural. The phrase 'wheels within wheels' has entered English idiom as a metaphor for hidden complexity; the chapter's literal meaning is mechanical — a description of an unusual wheel configuration that enables omnidirectional motion. The translation preserves the literal Hebrew with the *as if* hedging; commentary surfaces the mechanical reading.

terms.standardEnglish: like the colour of a beryl / like beryl / like chrysolite
terms.rationale: *Tarshish* (תַּרְשִׁישׁ) as a stone-name appears at Ex 28:20 (high priest's breastplate stones), Song 5:14, Dan 10:6, and here at Ezek 1:16. The standard English translations differ: KJV 'beryl', ASV 'beryl', NJPS 'beryl', NRSV 'chrysolite', ESV 'beryl'. Modern gemological identifications variously propose chrysolite, peridot, yellow jasper, or yellow beryl. The classical-era association is **golden-yellow / olive-gold** — a precious stone of luminous appearance. The Vulgate uses *chrysolithus*. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew form 'tarshish-stone' rather than picking one of the proposed English identifications — both because the gemological identification is uncertain and because the chapter has stacked three precious-substance descriptions on the apparatus (*chashmal* electrum-like, *nechoshet qalal* burnished bronze, *tarshish* golden-yellow stone), and the visual register consistency is worth preserving. The hedging *ke-ein* ('like the look of') is preserved as 'like the look of'. Reclassified as `inferred` because the choice to preserve the Hebrew (vs. picking 'beryl' or 'chrysolite') is a translator's interpretive call.

terms.standardEnglish: their rings were full of eyes
terms.wohChoice: the rims of the four of them were full of eyes round about
terms.rationale: *Gabbotam* — *their rims* (the outer-edge surfaces of the wheels) — *meleot einayim saviv* — *full of eyes round about*. The phrase has been read at every level. Literal: the wheels are conscious-perceiving entities, eyes distributed around their rims. Iconographic: gemstones glinting like eyes (the wheels described in the same precious-stone register as *chashmal*, *nechoshet qalal*, *tarshish*). Technical: sensors / detectors / aperture-like features distributed around the rim, the apparatus's perceptive-data-gathering surface. Mythological / angelic: the eye-motif appears in seraphim and cherubim iconography across ANE traditions; Revelation 4:6 re-uses Ezekiel's chayyot with the eye-multitude transferred to the chayyot themselves. // The chapter does not specify which register the eyes belong to. The WoH translation preserves the literal *einayim* (eyes) and the *saviv* (round about) — letting the reader meet the chapter's strangeness directly. Commentary surfaces the range of readings.

terms.literal: the ruach of the living-thing was in the wheels
terms.standardEnglish: the spirit of the living creature was in the wheels
terms.wohChoice: the ruach of the living creature was in the wheels
terms.rationale: Ezekiel 1's most explicit statement of the integrated-control-system principle (1:20, 1:21). *Ruach* — wind / breath / spirit (same three-fold lexical field as in Gen 1:2's *ruach-elohim*) — functions in Ezekiel 1 as the *animating directive* of the chayyot-and-wheels system. The chayyot move *wherever the ruach is to go* (1:12, 1:20); the wheels rise *with* the chayyot because the *ruach of the chayyah is in the wheels* (1:20, 1:21). // The chapter preserves a precise distinction: *chayyah* is singular (the living-thing, collective), *ba-ofannim* is plural (in the wheels). The system has *one* directive principle (singular ruach of chayyah) distributed across *multiple* parts (the four wheels). The chapter is describing an integrated control architecture. // The WoH translation preserves the noun *ruach* in its Hebrew form rather than picking 'wind' or 'spirit' prematurely — both because the chapter's *ruach* covers the spectrum and because the technical reading (an animating-coordinating force embedded in the apparatus) is best served by the preserved Hebrew term. Reclassified as `inferred` because the choice to preserve the Hebrew over translating is a translator's interpretive call.

terms.source: קוֹל כַּנְפֵיהֶם כְּקוֹל מַיִם רַבִּים כְּקוֹל־שַׁדַּי
terms.translit: qol kanfeihem k'qol mayim rabbim k'qol Shaddai
terms.literal: the sound of their wings like the sound of many waters, like the voice of Shaddai
terms.standardEnglish: the noise of their wings, like the noise of great waters, as the voice of the Almighty
terms.wohChoice: the sound of their wings like the sound of many waters, like the voice of Shaddai
terms.rationale: The chapter stacks four sound-similes for the wings in motion at v24: *qol mayim rabbim* (sound of many waters), *qol Shaddai* (voice of Shaddai), *qol hamullah* (tumultuous sound), *qol machaneh* (sound of an army-camp). The central simile is *qol Shaddai* — the wings make a sound *like the voice of Shaddai*. // *Shaddai* (שַׁדַּי) is the patriarchal-era divine name (Gen 17:1, 28:3, 35:11, 43:14, 48:3, 49:25; Ex 6:3 explicitly names *El Shaddai* as the name by which YHWH was known to the patriarchs). The conventional English translation is 'the Almighty' (via the LXX's παντοκράτωρ / pantokrator and the Vulgate's *omnipotens*) but the actual etymology is contested: possibly from *shadad* (destroy, overpower), from *shad* (breast, with maternal connotations), or from Akkadian *shadu* (mountain) — the latter favored by some modern scholars who read *Shaddai* as the divine epithet 'the One of the Mountain'. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew name *Shaddai* rather than the conventional 'Almighty', matching the project's pattern of preserving divine-names (YHWH, Elohim, El). The chapter is using *Shaddai* specifically (not YHWH) — this is a deliberate Hebrew Bible vocabulary choice and the WoH translation respects it.

terms.literal: like the appearance of sapphire stone, the likeness of a throne
terms.standardEnglish: as the appearance of a sapphire stone, the likeness of a throne
terms.wohChoice: like the appearance of sapphire stone, the likeness of a throne
terms.rationale: *Eben sappir* — *sapphire stone* — appears in the Hebrew Bible at Ex 24:10 (Moses and the seventy elders see at Sinai: *and there was under his feet as it were a paved work of sapphire stone, and as it were the very heaven for clearness*), Ezek 1:26, Ezek 10:1, and the closely-related stone in the high-priest's breastplate (Ex 28:18, 39:11). The Hebrew *sappir* may refer to *sapphire* (modern gemological identification) or to *lapis lazuli* (the deep-blue stone widely used in ANE iconography for divine-throne platforms — Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and Levantine art alike). // *Demut kisse* — *the likeness of a throne*. Ezekiel uses *demut* (likeness) rather than direct identification. The chapter is reusing the Sinai-theophany vocabulary: at Sinai, the elders saw blue-stone-platform *under his feet*; at the Kebar canal, Ezekiel sees blue-stone-throne *above the dome*. The structural parallel is part of the chapter's claim about what Ezekiel is seeing: the same kind of phenomenon as the Sinai theophany, but in exile, by a canal, in Babylonia.

*Kavod YHWH* (כְּבוֹד יְהוָה) — *the glory of YHWH* — is the Hebrew Bible's technical term for the *radiant, visible presence-manifestation* of YHWH. The term denotes a specific phenomenon: the cloud-and-fire of the Exodus that led Israel through the wilderness (Ex 13:21-22, 14:19-20, 16:10), the cloud-and-fire descent on Sinai (Ex 19:16-19, 24:15-18), the cloud-filled tabernacle (Ex 40:34-35), the cloud-filled Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11; 2 Chr 5:13-14, 7:1-3), Isaiah's throne-vision (Isa 6:1-3), and — climactically — Ezekiel's vision here (Ezek 1:28, 3:12, 3:23, 8:4, 9:3, 10:4, 10:18-19, 11:22-23, 43:2-5, 44:4). The Ezekiel book is structurally organized around the *movement* of the kavod — leaving the Temple in chapter 10, returning to the eschatological Temple in chapter 43. // The Hebrew root *kbd* covers *heaviness, weight, significance, honor, glory*. The standard translation 'glory' carries the abstract sense; the Hebrew preserves the concrete-physical dimension (something heavy and present, visible and weighty). The chapter's deployment at Ezek 1:28 — *mar'eh demut kvod YHWH* ('the appearance of the likeness of the glory of YHWH') — is the chapter's most-extended hedging formula: three layers of qualifying language (*mar'eh demut kavod*) before the divine name. Ezekiel is not asserting that he saw YHWH; he is identifying the chayyot-and-wheels-and-throne-and-figure as the *kavod-YHWH* — the technical presence-manifestation, not the divine essence directly. // The WoH translation preserves YHWH (not 'the LORD') and 'glory' (the conventional English for *kavod*), with commentary surfacing the technical-presence sense and the hedging formula structure.

*Keruvim* (כְּרוּבִים, plural of *keruv* כְּרוּב) — *cherubim* — are the canonical Hebrew Bible class of winged composite attendant-figures. They flank the Ark of the Covenant (Ex 25:18-20, 37:7-9), guard the way to the Tree of Life at Eden's eastern gate (Gen 3:24), and appear as architectural-iconographic figures in Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 6:23-28). Ezekiel 10 explicitly identifies the *chayyot* of chapter 1 with the *keruvim* (10:15, 10:20, 10:22) — making this one of the Hebrew Bible's most consequential cross-textual identifications. // Ezekiel 10 already uses the term *keruvim* freely from v1 onward, before the explicit identification at v15. The chapter assumes the reader will recognize the term from Hebrew Bible iconographic tradition. // The Christian-tradition rendering 'cherubim' (and singular 'cherub') is well-established and the WoH translation adopts it; alternative renderings (transliterated 'keruvim', or the more abstract 'guardian-figures') are defensible but would obscure the cross-textual recognition the chapter is staging. The English 'cherub' / 'cherubim' has — outside scholarly contexts — drifted into the European post-Renaissance art register (chubby winged children); the chapter's *keruv* is the composite winged-guardian throne-attendant of ANE iconography (winged sphinxes, winged bulls, winged lions). The WoH translation preserves the English 'cherubim' and lets commentary surface the iconographic register where helpful.

*Galgal* (גַּלְגַּל) is the Hebrew word for a rolling thing — a wheel-rim, a whirling object, a rolling-mass. The root is *gll* (to roll). Ezekiel 10 introduces the term explicitly: *la-ofannim lahem qora ha-galgal b'aznai* — *as for the wheels, they were called galgal in my hearing* (10:13). The chapter is documenting the alternative technical name for the *ofannim* of chapter 1 — what the prophet heard the heavenly attendants call the wheels. // Elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible the word denotes: a thunder-storm sound (Ps 77:18 — *qol galgal* — 'the sound of the rolling-thing'); the wheel at the well (Eccl 12:6 — *galgal ha-bor*); whirling chaff (Isa 17:13); the whirring of chariot-wheels (Jer 47:3); the wheel-of-fortune image (Sir 33:5). The lexical core is: a rolling, whirling, rotating object — wheel or whirlwind. // The chapter's vocabulary contribution is theologically consequential. *Galgal* becomes the foundational Hebrew Bible word for the **Merkavah** (chariot) mystical tradition — the rabbinic-mystical reading of Ezekiel 1 + 10 as a description of the divine chariot-throne system. The Hekhalot literature, *Shi'ur Qomah*, and the broader Jewish mystical tradition use *galgal* and its plural *galgalim* as load-bearing technical terms. The Raëlian canon's engagement with 'the Kabbalah' (see `Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon` wiki entry) implicitly engages this vocabulary cluster. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew word *galgal* untranslated — parallel to how *chashmal* and *Elohim* are preserved. The translation marks the chapter's vocabulary-introduction moment rather than papering over it.

*Ha-ish levush ha-badim* — *the man clothed in linen* — names a distinct figure who appears in the Ezekiel throne-vision narratives (Ezek 9:2, 9:3, 9:11, 10:2, 10:6, 10:7). The same figure appears in Daniel 10:5 and 12:6-7 (the *ish levush ha-badim* by the Tigris in Daniel's vision). // *Badim* (בַּדִּים, plural of *bad*) is the Hebrew term for **linen** as a cultic textile. Linen garments are specifically priestly: the high priest wears linen on Yom Kippur (Lev 16:4); the priesthood wear linen for Temple service (Ex 28:42, 39:28); the linen-clad figure in Dan 12:6-7 is iconographically a priestly-heavenly-attendant. The figure is implicitly identified as an angelic-priestly attendant — the heavenly counterpart of the earthly high priest. // The Ezekiel 9-10 narrative has the linen-clad man performing two distinct functions: (1) in chapter 9, marking the foreheads of the faithful (the *tav* mark, Ezek 9:4) before the executioners begin their work; (2) in chapter 10, taking fire from between the cherubim to scatter over Jerusalem (10:2, 10:6-7). Both functions are priestly-judicial: marking the elect, transmitting cultic-judicial fire. // The Christian tradition has identified the figure with Christ (the New Testament's *one like a son of man* of Rev 1:13-14 echoes the Ezekiel-Daniel linen-clad figure). The Hebrew gives the figure no name. The chapter does not specify his identity beyond the *clothed-in-linen* descriptor.

*Miftan* (מִפְתָּן) is the Hebrew word for *threshold* — the architectural transition-point between inside and outside, between sacred and profane spaces. The word appears in 1 Sam 5:4-5 (the Philistines' Dagon temple — Dagon falls on the *miftan*), Zeph 1:9, Ezek 9:3, 10:4, 10:18, 46:2, 47:1. // In Ezekiel 10, *miftan ha-bayit* (the threshold of the house) is the architectural locus where the *kavod-YHWH* pauses during its departure from the Temple (10:4 the kavod moves *onto* the threshold; 10:18 the kavod moves *off* the threshold). The chapter is making the departure architecturally-spatially concrete: the kavod moves from the holy-of-holies (above the cherubim of the Ark) → onto the threshold (the door of the house) → off the threshold (out of the house entirely). The threshold is the structural midpoint of the divine-presence withdrawal.

terms.source: וַיֵּצֵא כְּבוֹד יְהוָה / וַיָּרָם כְּבוֹד יְהוָה
terms.literal: and the glory of YHWH went out / rose up
terms.standardEnglish: and the glory of the LORD went forth / went up
terms.wohChoice: the glory of YHWH went out / rose up
terms.rationale: Ezekiel 10 stages the departure of the *kavod-YHWH* (see glossary entry `kavod-yhwh`) from the Jerusalem Temple. The chapter uses two distinct verbs of motion in sequence: // **v4**: *vayyaram kvod-YHWH me-al ha-keruv al miftan ha-bayit* — *the kavod-YHWH rose up from above the cherub onto the threshold of the house*. The verb is *ram* (to rise, be lifted up) — the kavod elevates from the holy-of-holies position toward the threshold. // **v18**: *vayyetze kvod-YHWH me-al miftan ha-bayit vayya'amod al ha-keruvim* — *the kavod-YHWH went out from above the threshold of the house and stood above the cherubim*. The verb is *yatza* (to go out, exit) — the kavod departs from the threshold and re-mounts the cherubim-apparatus. // **v19**: cherubim lift up, fly out the east gate; the kavod is *above them*. The chapter is staging a three-stage departure: holy-of-holies → threshold → above apparatus → exit. // Ezek 11:23 will note the next position: *vayya'al kvod-YHWH me-al tokh ha-ir vayya'amod al ha-har asher mi-qedem la-ir* — *the kavod-YHWH went up from the midst of the city and stood on the mountain that is east of the city* (the Mount of Olives). The full departure sequence: Temple → east gate → Mount of Olives east of Jerusalem. // The departure-vocabulary marks the chapter's theological-historical claim: the divine presence has *withdrawn* from the Temple ahead of the city's 587/586 BCE destruction. The chapter is the prophetic-textual record of the *divine abandonment* of Jerusalem. The WoH translation preserves the motion-verbs (*rose up*, *went out*) and the YHWH name (not 'the LORD').

*Elohei-Yisrael* (אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל) — *the Elohim of Israel* — is the patriarchal/covenantal compound divine name. The phrase first appears in Gen 33:20 (Jacob's altar at Shechem, *El-Elohei-Yisrael*), and recurs at Ex 5:1, Ex 24:10 (the elders see *Elohei-Yisrael* at Sinai), Josh 7:13, 1 Sam 23:10-11, and in liturgical-prophetic contexts throughout the Hebrew Bible. The compound is the specifically-covenantal naming — it ties *Elohim* to the people *Yisrael* in a relational-historical framework. // The standard English 'the God of Israel' uses the singular smoothing of *Elohim* the WoH translation explicitly rejects (see [`elohim-as-translation`](/wiki/elohim/)). The WoH translation preserves the plural class-noun rendering — *the Elohim of Israel* — matching the convention used throughout the WoH Genesis translation. // Ezekiel 10 invokes *Elohei-Yisrael* twice (10:19, 10:20) precisely at the departure moment: the *kavod Elohei-Yisrael* (the glory of the Elohim of Israel) is what leaves the Temple. The covenantal name is being invoked at the covenantal-undoing moment — the chapter is theologically loaded.

*Biq'ah* (בִּקְעָה) is the Hebrew word for an open, flat valley or plain — distinct from *gai* (narrow ravine) or *emek* (broader valley between hills). The same noun is used in Gen 11:2 for the *biq'ah* in the land of Shinar where Babel was built, in Isa 40:4 for the valleys-to-be-raised in the wilderness highway image, and in Zech 12:11 for the *biq'at Megiddo* (the Megiddo plain). // The chapter places the bone-field in a *biq'ah* — an open, flat staging-area large enough to host *very many* bones (*rabbot me'od*, v2) and to serve as the site of a procedure that will produce an *exceedingly great army* (*chayil gadol me'od me'od*, v10). The chapter is staging the vision on a *staging-ground*, the topographic feature historically used for mass-assembly. The KJV/ASV 'valley' is acceptable; the WoH translation preserves it.

*Atzamot* (עֲצָמוֹת, plural of *etzem*) is the standard Hebrew word for *bones* — the skeletal-anatomical sense. The same root (*atzam*) also covers the metaphorical *substance, essence, self* (cf. *etzem ha-yom ha-zeh* — 'on that very day'). // *Yeveshot* (יְבֵשׁוֹת) is the feminine plural adjective of *yavesh* (dry, withered, dried up) — the same root that names the *yabashah* (dry land) of Gen 1:9-10. The chapter pairs the two as a fixed phrase: *atzamot yeveshot* — *dry bones*. // The phrase becomes the chapter's keyword. *Atzamot yeveshot* recurs at 37:2, 4, 11. The Wheel of Heaven translation preserves the standard English 'dry bones' — the rendering is grammatically straightforward and the literal-physical sense is what the chapter requires.

terms.literal: son of human / son of Adam
terms.rationale: *Ben-adam* (בֶּן־אָדָם) — literally *son of human / son of Adam* — is the prophet Ezekiel's standard appellation throughout the book. The compound combines *ben* (son) + *adam* (human, the category-noun established in Gen 1-3). It appears **93 times** across Ezekiel — more than any other Hebrew Bible figure receives any single appellation. // The Hebrew Bible elsewhere uses *ben-adam* as a poetic-emphatic for *mortal, human-being* (Ps 8:5 — *ma enosh ki tizk'rennu u-ven-adam ki tifq'dennu* — 'what is humankind that you should remember it, and the son-of-human that you should attend to it'; cf. Ps 80:18, 144:3; Job 25:6, 35:8). The Aramaic equivalent *bar-enash* in Dan 7:13 becomes the New Testament Greek ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου (*ho huios tou anthrōpou*) — *the son of man* — Jesus' favored self-designation in the Gospels. // The WoH translation renders *ben-adam* as **'son of human'** rather than the conventional 'son of man'. Two reasons: (1) the WoH glossary has already established `ha-adam` as *the human* (rather than *the man*) — preserving the category-noun sense from Gen 1:26-27 rather than the gendered 'man'; (2) *ben-adam* is structurally about *being-in-the-human-class* (the prophet as a member of the *adam*-category that was made in the image-and-likeness, not as a male human). The rendering matches the WoH project's broader *adam*-as-human convention; 'son of human' is grammatically natural in English and preserves the category-register the Hebrew has.

*Adonai YHWH* (אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה) — the compound divine name. *Adonai* (literally 'my lords' — plural-of-majesty of *adon*, master/lord) was the traditional Jewish liturgical substitute for the Tetragrammaton YHWH after the 3rd-century-BCE practice of not pronouncing the divine name in everyday speech. The Masoretic vocalization of the Tetragrammaton in *Adonai YHWH* compounds uses the vowel-points of *Elohim* (יְהוִה, with the *e-i* of Elohim rather than the *e-o-a* of Adonai itself) — a Masoretic device for indicating 'do not pronounce this YHWH as Adonai (because Adonai already precedes it) — pronounce it Elohim instead'. // The conventional English KJV rendering — *the Lord GOD* (with *GOD* in small caps representing YHWH) — is the historical compromise. The WoH translation preserves the actual Hebrew: *Adonai* renders as *Lord*, *YHWH* stays as the divine name *YHWH* (not 'the LORD' substitution). The result — *Lord YHWH* — surfaces both vocabulary elements directly. // The Adonai-YHWH compound is one of Ezekiel's signature divine-address registers (occurs >215 times across the book, more frequent than in any other Hebrew Bible book).

The chapter names a precise sequence of biological-construction stages in 37:6: *gidim* (sinews, tendons, connective tissue) → *basar* (flesh, soft tissue) → *`or* (skin, integument) → *ruach* (animating principle, inserted last). The sequence is anatomically-procedurally ordered: structural framework (bones, already in place) + connective tissue + soft tissue + outer integument + animation. // *Gidim* (גִּדִים, plural of *gid*) is the standard Hebrew noun for *sinew/tendon* — anatomically the fibrous tissue connecting muscle to bone; the noun also appears in Gen 32:33 (the *gid ha-nasheh* — sciatic-nerve sinew of Jacob's wrestling injury). // *Basar* (בָּשָׂר) is the broad-spectrum noun *flesh* — covering muscle, soft tissue, and (by extension) flesh-as-creaturely-substance (the phrase *kol basar* = 'all flesh', used throughout the Flood narrative). // *`Or* (עוֹר) is the noun for *skin* — both human skin and animal hide (Gen 3:21 *kotnot `or* — 'tunics of skin'); the chapter's use is unambiguously the integumentary covering of a body. // The Raëlian canon, in *Extra-Terrestrials Took Me To Their Planet*, reads this verse as a description of biological-creation procedure: the chapter's vocabulary (sinews-then-flesh-then-skin-then-ruach) is procedurally precise and matches the technical-sequential description of body-assembly. The WoH translation preserves the procedural language without inserting interpretive content; commentary at v6 surfaces the canon's reading and the textual data that supports it.

terms.standardEnglish: from the four winds, come, breath
terms.wohChoice: from the four ruchot, come, ruach
terms.rationale: Ezek 37:9 calls the animating-principle from four directions: *me-arba ruchot bo'i ha-ruach* — *from the four ruchot, come, ruach*. The verse uses *ruach* (wind / breath / spirit) twice in a single clause: singular *ha-ruach* (the animating principle being summoned), plural *arba ruchot* (the four winds / four cardinal directions). // The *arba ruchot* (four winds) is the Hebrew Bible's directional convention — the four corners, the four winds, the four directions (cf. Jer 49:36, Zech 2:6 / 2:10, Dan 7:2, Dan 8:8, Dan 11:4). The ruach is summoned from all four cardinal directions simultaneously to enter the assembled bodies. // The WoH translation preserves *ruach* in both occurrences as the Hebrew technical noun, consistent with the chapter's use of *ruach* throughout (cf. v 1, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 14). The singular-plural ambiguity in Hebrew is preserved in English by repeating the same noun form (lower-case ruach for singular, lower-case ruchot for plural — both transliterated).

terms.source: וַיִּחְיוּ וַיַּעַמְדוּ עַל־רַגְלֵיהֶם חַיִל גָּדוֹל מְאֹד־מְאֹד
terms.translit: vayichyu vaya'amdu al-raglehem chayil gadol me'od me'od
terms.literal: and they lived and stood on their feet — an exceedingly very great army
terms.standardEnglish: they lived, and stood upon their feet, an exceeding great army
terms.wohChoice: they lived, and they stood up on their feet — an exceedingly great army
terms.rationale: Ezek 37:10's closing image: the animated bodies stand up *al-raglehem* (on their feet) — *chayil gadol me'od me'od* — *an exceedingly great army*. // *Vayichyu* (qal of *chayah* — to live) names the procedural-completion: the bodies *come-to-life*. *Vaya'amdu al-raglehem* (and they stood on their feet) names the postural-functional completion: the bodies achieve standing posture, indicating muscular-neural integration is complete. // *Chayil* (חַיִל) is the standard Hebrew noun for *army, military force, host* (cf. Ex 14:4, 2 Kgs 6:14, Ps 33:16). The chapter is staging the bodies as having a military-functional organization — not a chaotic crowd but a structured force. The doubled *me'od me'od* (very very, exceedingly very) is the chapter's intensifier-of-scale: a vast force.

Ezek 37:12-13 expands the vision from valley-of-bones to grave-opening. *Hinneh ani poteach et-qivrotekhem* — *behold, I am opening your graves*. // The verb form is *poteach* — present-active participle, *currently-opening* — naming the action as already in process, not merely promised for the future. The chapter is staging the grave-opening as *underway*. // *Qivrotekhem* (קִבְרוֹתֵיכֶם) is the plural-2mp possessive of *qever* (grave, tomb, burial place). The same noun appears throughout the Hebrew Bible (Gen 23:9, 23:20 for Abraham's purchased burial cave; 2 Kgs 22:20 for Josiah's grave). The chapter is staging a *mass grave-opening* — a population-wide procedure of grave-opening and re-emergence.

terms.source: עֵץ יוֹסֵף ... עֵץ יְהוּדָה ... לְעֵץ אֶחָד
terms.translit: ets Yosef ... ets Yehudah ... l'ets echad
terms.literal: the stick of Joseph ... the stick of Judah ... into one stick
terms.standardEnglish: the stick of Joseph ... the stick of Judah ... one stick
terms.wohChoice: the stick of Joseph ... the stick of Judah ... into a single stick
terms.rationale: The two-sticks sign-act of Ezek 37:15-28. *Ets Yosef* (the stick labeled for Joseph — representing the northern kingdom, alternately called Joseph or Ephraim) and *Ets Yehudah* (the stick labeled for Judah — representing the southern kingdom centered on Jerusalem). The two are to be joined into *ets echad* (a single stick), prefiguring the political reunification of the divided Israelite kingdom. // *Ets* (עֵץ) is the standard Hebrew noun for *tree, wood, stick*. In this context the chapter is specifying *wooden sticks* / *staves* — physical objects the prophet handles. The English 'stick' is the standard rendering; some translations use 'piece of wood' (NIV) or 'staff' (Wycliffe). The WoH translation uses 'stick' for the concrete object-sense the chapter requires. // The sign-act predicts the eschatological reunification of the divided Israelite kingdom (split since 922 BCE; the northern kingdom destroyed in 722 BCE, the southern kingdom destroyed in 587 BCE; never historically reunified). The chapter is forecasting a future-state restoration that did not historically occur — making the verse a *messianic-eschatological* prediction in standard Hebrew Bible prophetic register.

terms.literal: covenant of peace ... my dwelling ... my sanctuary
terms.standardEnglish: covenant of peace ... my tabernacle ... my sanctuary
terms.wohChoice: covenant of peace ... my dwelling ... my sanctuary
terms.rationale: The closing covenant-vocabulary cluster in Ezek 37:26-28. // *Brit shalom* (בְּרִית שָׁלוֹם) — *covenant of peace* — names the post-restoration covenant explicitly as one of *shalom* (peace, wholeness, completeness). The phrase appears elsewhere at Num 25:12 (the priestly covenant with Phinehas), Isa 54:10, Mal 2:5. The chapter is naming the eschatological-restoration covenant in continuity with prior peace-covenant traditions. // *Mishkan* (מִשְׁכָּן) — *dwelling-place, tabernacle* — is the technical term for the portable wilderness sanctuary of Exodus (Ex 25-31, 35-40). The chapter promises *mishkani aleihem* (my dwelling above them) — YHWH's mishkan-presence will be over the restored people. The Christian-tradition rendering 'tabernacle' is technically correct but obscures the *dwelling* sense the Hebrew preserves; the WoH translation prefers 'dwelling' as the broader-applicable English term. // *Miqdash* (מִקְדָּשׁ) — *sanctuary, holy place* — names the architectural-cultic complex where YHWH's presence is encountered. The chapter promises a *miqdash* in the midst of the restored people, set *forever* (*l'olam*). // The cluster together names the eschatological-restoration end-state: a permanent covenant of peace, with YHWH's dwelling-presence in a permanent sanctuary among the unified people. The chapter closes the Ezekiel-triptych on this restoration-vision — the inverse of the kavod-departure of chapter 10.

***Ha-satan*** (הַשָּׂטָן) is the Hebrew Bible's most-contested single noun. The Hebrew preserves the **definite article** *ha* — *the satan*, *the adversary* — making this a **functional role, not a proper name**. The Christian-tradition rendering 'Satan' (capitalized, proper-name) loses the article and personifies what the Hebrew presents as a council-role. // The lexical core: *satan* (שָׂטָן) is the standard Hebrew noun for *adversary, accuser, prosecutor*. Same root as the verb *satan* (oppose, accuse). The noun appears across the Hebrew Bible: Num 22:22, 32 (YHWH's malakh standing as a *satan* — adversary — in Balaam's path); 1 Sam 29:4 (David called a potential *satan* to the Philistines); 2 Sam 19:23 (Abishai called a *satan* to David); 1 Kgs 5:18, 11:14, 11:23, 11:25 (political adversaries); Ps 109:6 (a satan standing at the right hand of the accused). In none of these is *satan* a proper name; it is a functional role. // The Hebrew Bible has only **three contexts** where *satan* appears with the definite article: Job 1-2, Zech 3:1-2 (*ha-satan* standing at the right hand of the high priest Joshua to accuse him), and the implied parallel of 1 Chr 21:1 (where *satan* notably *lacks* the article — possibly transitional to proper-name usage). In Job and Zechariah, the figure is **a council member** functioning as adversarial-prosecutor; in 1 Chronicles 21:1 the grammar is ambiguous. // The figure of *Satan-the-devil* in later Christian theology is built up over centuries from disparate sources (the serpent of Gen 3, the morning-star of Isa 14, the prince of Tyre of Ezek 28, the dragon of Rev 12, the diabolos of the NT). The Hebrew Bible's *ha-satan* of Job 1-2 is **not yet this figure**. The chapter stages ha-satan as: (1) a member of the *bnei ha-elohim* divine council (1:6, 2:1 — *gam ha-satan b'tokham*, *the satan also came among them*); (2) operating under YHWH's delegated authority (1:12, 2:6 — YHWH grants permission and sets the limits); (3) functioning as adversarial-prosecutor or tester (the role-function of the council). The figure is **adversarial-but-not-adversarial-to-YHWH** — adversarial *to Job* in the test-function, but operating *within* YHWH's authority. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew article — **'the adversary'** (lowercase, with definite article). This matches the modern scholarly consensus: NJPS Tanakh ('the Adversary'), Clines's Anchor Bible commentary, Habel's OTL commentary, Newsom in the New Interpreter's Bible, Greenberg, Mettinger. The translation does not commit to the proper-name reading and does not pre-empt the reader's encounter with the chapter's actual figure. // Reclassified as `inferred` because the choice between 'the adversary' (preserving the Hebrew article and role-function) and 'Satan' (the Christian-tradition retrojection) is a genuine interpretive call. The lexical evidence and the modern scholarly consensus support the role-function reading; the proper-name reading is the standard English-tradition default. The WoH translation goes with the role-function reading and surfaces the choice in commentary. // The Wheel of Heaven lens reads ha-satan as a council-internal adversarial-tester — a function within the divine governance, not an autonomous evil counterforce. This is the modern divine-council reading (Heiser's *The Unseen Realm*; the classic Mesopotamian *mukallimu* — interrogator/prosecutor — parallel) and is consistent with the WoH framing of the divine system as multi-figure-with-internal-functions.

terms.standardEnglish: to present themselves before the LORD
terms.rationale: *L'hityatzev* (לְהִתְיַצֵּב) is the hithpael infinitive of *yatzav* — *to take a stand, station oneself, present oneself*. The hithpael denotes reflexive-formal action: the bnei ha-elohim *present themselves* (place themselves) before YHWH. // The phrase appears at Job 1:6, 2:1 as a **periodic-court-assembly** formula. The same verbal form recurs in Hebrew Bible contexts of formal presentation: Num 11:16 (the seventy elders presenting themselves at the Tent of Meeting), Deut 31:14 (Moses and Joshua presenting themselves), Josh 24:1 (Israel presenting itself at Shechem), 1 Sam 10:19 (Israel presenting itself for Saul's coronation), 2 Chr 11:13 (the priests presenting themselves to Rehoboam). The verb's standard semantic field is **court-presentation** — appearing before a sovereign or authority. // The Job 1:6 and 2:1 verses are staging a **periodic divine-council assembly**: the bnei ha-elohim come to present themselves before YHWH on a recurring basis. The chapter is positioning this as standard procedure — *vay'hi ha-yom* (the day came) opens the scene as a *day-when-this-happens*, implying repetition. The WoH translation preserves the formal-court register and the YHWH name (not 'the LORD').

terms.literal: from roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it
terms.standardEnglish: from going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it
terms.wohChoice: from roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it
terms.rationale: Ha-satan's self-description at Job 1:7 and 2:2. Two verbs of locomotion: *shut* (rove, range, traverse) and *hithallekh* (hithpael of *halakh* — walk-about, walk habitually). // The hithpael *mit'hallekh* / *hithallekh* is the chapter's most pointed lexical echo. The same verbal form is used of: YHWH-Elohim *walking about* in the garden of Eden (Gen 3:8 — *mit'hallekh ba-gan*), Enoch *walking about* with the Elohim (Gen 5:22, 24 — *vayit'hallekh Chanokh et ha-Elohim*), Noah *walking about* with the Elohim (Gen 6:9 — *et ha-Elohim hit'hallekh Noach*), and divine-presence-imagery in 2 Sam 7:6-7, Lev 26:12, Deut 23:15. The verb denotes habitual, at-home, frequent movement-in-residence. // Ha-satan walks-about *on the earth* — in the same verbal form as the patriarchs walked-about *with the Elohim*. The chapter is positioning ha-satan as having the earth as its movement-domain in the same residential-habitual register as the antediluvian-favored figures had divine company as theirs. The function is **territorial-investigative** — ranging the earth, presumably gathering information for the council. The verbal echo across Gen 3:8, 5:22-24, 6:9 and Job 1:7, 2:2 is one of the Hebrew Bible's quieter cross-textual linkages. // The WoH translation preserves the standard rendering 'roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it' — the language is straightforward; the lexical echo is for the commentary to surface.

terms.literal: a man there was in the land of Uz
terms.standardEnglish: there was a man in the land of Uz
terms.wohChoice: a man there was in the land of Uz
terms.rationale: *Eretz Uz* — *the land of Uz* — is the geographic-cultural setting of the book of Job. Uz is identified in Gen 10:23 as descended from Aram (Shem's line), in Gen 22:21 as Nahor's eldest son, and in Gen 36:28 as a Horite/Edomite figure. The location is generally placed east of the Jordan, possibly in northern Arabia or Edom (Lam 4:21 locates 'the daughter of Edom dwelling in the land of Uz'). // The chapter is staging Job's story **outside Israel** — among the Aramean / Edomite peripheries, in a non-Israelite cultural sphere. This is consistent with the book's broader Wisdom-literature register (cf. the Aramean / Edomite identifications of Job's three friends at 2:11). The book is positioning itself as **international Wisdom literature** — cognate with Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and broader ANE wisdom-tradition texts. // The WoH translation preserves *Uz* as the Hebrew form (matching the project's general preservation of toponyms). The verb-noun-noun word order in the Hebrew opening (*ish hayah b'eretz Uz Iyov shmo* — 'a man there was in the land of Uz, Job by name') is the standard folktale-opening register; the WoH rendering preserves the inverted opening rather than smoothing to 'There was a man'.

The chapter's signature characterization of Job: *tam v'yashar yere Elohim v'sar me-ra* — *blameless and upright, one who feared Elohim and turned from evil*. The pair recurs at 1:1, 1:8, 2:3 — each time as the affirmation of Job's standing. // *Tam* (תָּם) — from *tom* (completeness, integrity, wholeness) — is **not 'perfect' in the moral-absolute sense**. The KJV/ASV rendering 'perfect' is misleading in modern English (which reads 'perfect' as flawless / mistake-free). The Hebrew *tam* is closer to **'blameless / whole / unblemished'** — the same root that names the ritually-unblemished sacrificial animals (*tamim*, *temimah*). Job is not asserted as morally-perfect; he is asserted as *whole / blameless / without compromising flaw*. // *Yashar* (יָשָׁר) — *upright, straight* — is the ethical-conduct twin of *tam*. The pair together names character (*tam* — internal wholeness) + conduct (*yashar* — external straightness). The Job-formula is the canonical Wisdom-literature character-affirmation. // The WoH translation goes with 'blameless and upright' (matching NJPS, NRSV, modern scholarly translations) — drops 'perfect' (KJV/ASV) which mis-modernizes.

YHWH refers to Job as *avdi* — *my servant* — at Job 1:8 and 2:3. The designation is canonical Hebrew Bible high-status: *avdi* is used of Moses (Num 12:7-8, Deut 34:5, Josh 1:2, Mal 4:4), David (2 Sam 7:5, 1 Kgs 11:13, 1 Kgs 14:8, Isa 37:35), the Patriarchs (Ex 32:13 — *Abraham, Yitzchak, Yisrael avadekha*), Isaiah's Suffering Servant (Isa 52:13), Zerubbabel (Hag 2:23), and a small set of other figures of singular covenantal-relational status. // The chapter positions Job in this elite category. The chapter is staging Job as a figure of high covenantal status by YHWH's own designation — an *avdi* alongside Moses, David, Abraham. // The standard English 'my servant Job' is the canonical rendering; the WoH translation preserves it.

terms.standardEnglish: for nothing / for nought / without cause
terms.rationale: The book's keystone single word at Job 1:9 — ha-satan's accusation: *ha-chinnam yare Iyov Elohim* — *does Job fear Elohim for nothing?* The word *chinnam* (חִנָּם, from the root *chen* — favor, grace) means *gratis, for nothing, without compensation, without cause*. // The word appears in many Hebrew Bible contexts: Gen 29:15 (Laban: 'should you serve me *for nothing*?'), Ex 21:2, 11 (the freed slave goes out *chinnam*), Num 11:5 (the fish 'we ate in Egypt *chinnam*' — without cost), Ezek 6:10, 14:23 (YHWH's actions are not *chinnam*), Ps 35:7, 19, 69:5, 109:3, 119:161 (those who hate without cause), Prov 1:11, 1:17, 3:30, 23:29, 24:28, 26:2 (gratuitous violence, gratuitous beating). The semantic field clusters around *transactional vs. gratuitous* and *with-reason vs. without-reason*. // The word returns in 2:3, this time with a reversed application: YHWH says ha-satan *incited me against him to swallow him chinnam* — *without cause*. The chapter's wordplay turns on this reversal: ha-satan accused Job of being righteous *chinnam* (transactional, i.e., not really gratuitous — paid); YHWH accuses ha-satan of being adversarial *chinnam* (gratuitously, without cause). The same word reverses orientation between the two parties. // The book's broader theological argument turns on the question of unconditional vs. transactional relationship. *Chinnam* is the keyword the book uses to mark the issue.

terms.source: וּבֵרֲכוּ אֱלֹהִים בִּלְבָבָם / יְבָרֲכֶךָּ / בָּרֵךְ אֱלֹהִים וָמֻת
terms.translit: u-verakhu Elohim bilvavam / y'varakhekka / barekh Elohim va-mut
terms.literal: and they blessed Elohim in their hearts (= cursed)
terms.rationale: The book of Job uses the verb *barakh* (בָּרַךְ — bless) as a **euphemism for *qillel*/*arar* (curse)** in four passages: 1:5, 1:11, 2:5, 2:9. The reverential-substitution device is well-attested in the Hebrew Bible: when the text needs to put curse-language next to *Elohim* or *YHWH*, the scribal tradition substitutes *barakh* (bless) for the literal curse-verb. The substitution makes the meaning clear from context — the audience is meant to read 'bless' but understand 'curse'. // The same scribal device appears at 1 Kgs 21:10, 13 (Naboth allegedly *blessed* — i.e., cursed — Elohim and the king); Ps 10:3 (where the LXX reads *bless*, the Hebrew context implies *curse*). The Talmudic tradition (b. Berakhot 5b) explicitly identifies the device as a *tikkun soferim* (scribal correction) — a deliberate substitution to avoid placing curse-words next to divine references. // The WoH translation handles the euphemism case-by-case: where the literal *barakh* (bless) is what the chapter means (e.g., Job 1:21 *y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh* — *may the name of YHWH be blessed*), the rendering is 'bless'. Where the chapter is using *barakh* as the curse-euphemism (1:5, 1:11, 2:5, 2:9), the rendering is 'curse' — restoring what the text actually means — with commentary surfacing the scribal-euphemistic device. // This is one of the few places in the WoH translation where the chosen English **differs from the literal Hebrew verb** — driven by the textual tradition's own evidence about the device.

terms.literal: fire of Elohim fell from the skies
terms.standardEnglish: fire of God fell from heaven
terms.wohChoice: fire of Elohim fell from the skies
terms.rationale: *Esh Elohim* (fire of Elohim) is the Hebrew Bible's designation for **divinely-sent fire** — lightning, supernatural conflagration, or fire-of-divine-origin in general. The phrase appears in 1 Kgs 18:38 (the fire that consumes Elijah's sacrifice on Mount Carmel), 2 Kgs 1:10, 12 (the fire Elijah calls down on the captains of fifties), Job 1:16 (here). The category-noun *Elohim* in the compound functions as an intensifier-genitive: 'God-fire' = fire of divine origin / divinely-sent fire. // The chapter at Job 1:16 names the second disaster as *esh Elohim nafelah min ha-shamayim* — *fire of Elohim fell from the skies*. The verb *nafal* (fall) and the source *min ha-shamayim* (from the skies) name a **descent-from-above fire phenomenon** — consistent with lightning or atmospheric-electrical discharge. The WoH translation preserves the literal compound (esh + Elohim) and the YHWH-translation convention; the commentary surfaces the lightning / meteorological reading.

terms.literal: a great wind came from beyond the desert
terms.standardEnglish: a great wind came from the wilderness
terms.wohChoice: a great wind came from beyond the desert
terms.rationale: Job 1:19 names the fourth disaster as a *ruach gedolah* — a *great wind* — coming *me-ever ha-midbar* (from beyond the desert). The compound *ruach + gedolah* uses the same root *ruach* as the Gen 1:2 cosmogonic *ruach Elohim* and the Ezekiel-vision *ruach-chayyot* — but here in the *wind*-register of the noun's three-fold lexical field (breath / wind / spirit). // The verse describes a **localized strong wind-event**: it came from across the desert, struck the four corners of the house simultaneously, brought the house down on the occupants. The combination of *coming from beyond the desert*, *striking all four corners at once*, and *causing total structural collapse* fits a downburst or strong desert windstorm (khamsin / sharqi). The chapter is naming a *meteorological* disaster — natural-causal, but described in vocabulary that surfaces the *ruach* etymological cluster the book of Job will return to in chapters 26, 27, 38. // *Me-ever ha-midbar* — *from beyond the desert* — names the wind's directional origin. The desert (*midbar*) is the southern/eastern wilderness; the wind is coming from across it (i.e., from further east — the Arabian or Mesopotamian quarter).

terms.source: יְהוָה נָתַן וַיהוָה לָקָח יְהִי שֵׁם יְהוָה מְבֹרָךְ
terms.translit: YHWH natan v'YHWH laqach — y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh
terms.literal: YHWH gave and YHWH has taken — may the name of YHWH be blessed
terms.standardEnglish: the LORD gave and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD
terms.wohChoice: YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken; may the name of YHWH be blessed
terms.rationale: Job's first-test response at 1:21 — the book's most-quoted line. *YHWH natan v'YHWH laqach* — *YHWH gave and YHWH has taken*. The two clauses are perfectly balanced: the same divine subject (YHWH), the opposite actions (*natan* — gave, *laqach* — took), the same simple syntax. // The chapter's keystone theological claim: Job acknowledges that the same agent who *gave* now *takes*. The acknowledgment is unconditional — neither the giving nor the taking changes Job's orientation. The response closes with *y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh* — *may the name of YHWH be blessed* — using *barakh* in its **strict-positive sense** (here genuinely *blessed*, not the euphemism-for-curse of v5, v11). // The verse is the precise inverse of the adversary's prediction (v11): the adversary expected Job to *curse* (using *barakh* as euphemism); Job actually *blesses* (using *barakh* in its strict sense). The verbal symmetry across vv 11 and 21 is the chapter's signature rhetorical move — the same Hebrew verb *barakh* used twice with opposite intended meanings, one euphemism, one strict-positive. // The WoH translation preserves YHWH (not 'the LORD') and the balanced parallel structure.

Ha-satan's proverb at Job 2:4: *or be'ad or v'khol asher la-ish yiten be'ad nafsho* — *skin for skin — all that a person has, he will give for his life*. The Hebrew preposition *be'ad* (for, on behalf of, in exchange for) sets up an exchange-relationship. // The proverb has been read in two main ways: (1) *self-preservation reading*: skin (Job's body) is what he'll give in exchange for his life — i.e., he'll sacrifice anything *up to* his own bodily integrity to preserve his life; (2) *substitution reading*: one sheds the skin of others (children, servants) to save one's own skin — i.e., Job tolerated the first test because the losses were external; he'll break when the test reaches his own body. // Both readings converge on the same prediction: the next test must reach Job's body. The chapter's argument-structure is escalating from *what Job has* to *what Job is*. // The proverb *or be'ad or* is **not** securely attested in any other ANE source. The construction is unique in the Hebrew Bible. The chapter is staging the adversary as using folk-wisdom that the audience would recognize — but modern scholars cannot fully reconstruct the proverb's standard usage outside this context. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'skin for skin' and lets the commentary surface the two reading-tracks.

terms.source: שְׁחִין רָע מִכַּף רַגְלוֹ וְעַד קָדְקֳדוֹ
terms.translit: shechin ra mi-kaf raglo v'ad qodqodo
terms.literal: severe sores from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head
terms.standardEnglish: sore boils from the sole of his foot unto his crown
terms.wohChoice: severe sores from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head
terms.rationale: Job 2:7 names the second-test affliction as *shechin ra* — *severe sores*. *Shechin* (שְׁחִין) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for **severe inflammatory skin afflictions**. The term appears at Ex 9:9-11 (the sixth Egyptian plague — *shechin* breaking out on humans and beasts), Lev 13:18-23 (priestly diagnosis of skin disease in the holiness code), Deut 28:27, 35 (curses of the covenant), 2 Kgs 20:7 / Isa 38:21 (Hezekiah's affliction). // The exact dermatological identification has been debated extensively. Proposals include: smallpox, severe boils, pemphigus, infected eruption, generalized impetigo, leprosy (in the old broad sense). The chapter does not specify the medical-diagnostic detail. The qualifier *ra* (bad, severe, evil) intensifies — *shechin ra* is the affliction at its worst grade. // *Mi-kaf raglo v'ad qodqodo* — *from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head* — is the standard Hebrew Bible totality-formula (cf. Deut 28:35, 2 Sam 14:25, Isa 1:6). The affliction is bodily-comprehensive: every inch of Job's skin is covered.

terms.source: גַּם אֶת־הַטּוֹב נְקַבֵּל מֵאֵת הָאֱלֹהִים וְאֶת־הָרָע לֹא נְקַבֵּל
terms.translit: gam et ha-tov n'qabel me-et ha-Elohim v'et ha-ra lo n'qabel
terms.literal: shall we accept the good from the Elohim and not accept the bad
terms.standardEnglish: shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?
terms.wohChoice: should we accept good from the Elohim and not accept bad?
terms.rationale: Job's second-test response at 2:10. The rhetorical question is the chapter's keystone theological-philosophical move: the symmetry of acceptance is required by the relationship. If you accept the good as coming from a single source, you accept the bad as coming from the same source. // The verbal pair *tov v-ra* (good and bad) is the **same vocabulary cluster** as Gen 2:9, 2:17 (the tree of *da'at tov v-ra* — *knowing good and bad*) and Gen 3:5, 3:22 (the human became like the Elohim, *yode'ei tov v-ra* — *knowing good and bad*). The chapter is invoking the Edenic-vocabulary cluster: humans *know good and bad*. The capacity to know good and bad — acquired in Gen 3 — is here applied. Job *knows* both, and accepts both. // The verb *n'qabel* (we shall accept, from *qibel* — receive, accept) is the chapter's standing-claim verb. Job is not endorsing the bad; he is accepting it **as coming from the same source as the good**. The chapter is making a sophisticated theological-philosophical move: the categorial unity of good and bad in the divine economy. // *Me-et ha-Elohim* (from the Elohim, with the definite article *ha*) — Job refers to *the Elohim* (the divine source, as a class-noun with definite article — matching the WoH project's general convention). The translation preserves *the Elohim*.
