The same noun, the same construction with *al* (upon), the same subject (YHWH), the same object (Pharaoh and Mitsrayim). // **The Abram-Pharaoh / Moses-Pharaoh parallel structure** can be tabulated cleanly: famine drives Israel down to Mitsrayim → Pharaoh oppresses (taking Sarai here; enslavement in Exodus) → YHWH afflicts Pharaoh with plagues (*n'ga'im g'dolim* / *n'ga'im*) → Pharaoh summons the Hebrew patriarch / prophet (v 18 here; Exod 12:31 there) → Pharaoh expels the Hebrews (v 20 here; Exod 12:33 there) → the Hebrews depart with great substance (vv 16, 20 — the gift-list; Exod 12:35–36 — the spoiling of the Egyptians). The structural parallel is extensive enough that critical scholarship (**Garbini, *History and Ideology in Ancient Israel*, 1988**; **Crüsemann, *The Torah*, 1992**; **Sarna, *JPS Torah Commentary*, 1989**) treats Gen 12:10–20 as a **deliberate prefiguration** of the Exodus, composed or redacted in light of the later narrative. // **Two readings of the parallel** in critical scholarship: (a) **typological redaction** — Gen 12:10-20 was composed late, modeled on the Exodus narrative, and inserted into the Abrahamic cycle to establish Abram as proto-Moses and the patriarchal descent-and-return as proto-Exodus; (b) **convergent type-scene** — both narratives draw on a common topos of foreign-court appropriation-and-deliverance, with no necessary direction of dependence. Most modern critical scholars (Westermann, Sarna, Alter) favor (a). // The WoH translation reads 'great plagues' matching ASV — preserves the cognate link to Exodus. The claim_type is *inferred* because the proto-Exodus reading is a *scholarly inference* from the verbal parallels — well-established in the consensus but a literary-critical synthesis rather than an explicit claim of either text.

terms.literal: and they sent him away / dismissed him / expelled him
terms.rationale: **The chapter closes with the verb that will close the Exodus.** // The verb *shalach* in the piel stem (*shillach*) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for **expulsion / sending-out / dismissal** of persons. The piel intensifies the qal *shalach* (send) toward forced removal: not merely *send* but *send off, dismiss, expel, set free*. The same piel verb governs the keystone Exodus moments: // **Exodus 6:1** — YHWH's promise to Moses that Pharaoh will send Israel out: *b'yad chazaqah y'shalchem u-v'yad chazaqah y'gar'shem me-artzo* — *with a strong hand he will send them out (y'shalchem), and with a strong hand he will drive them out from his land*. // **Exodus 11:1** — YHWH's announcement of the tenth plague: *acharei khen y'shalach et'khem mi-zeh, k'shalcho kalah garesh y'garesh et'khem mi-zeh* — *after that he will send you out (y'shalach) from here; when he sends you out completely (k'shalcho kalah), he will utterly drive you out from here*. // **Exodus 12:33** — the immediate aftermath of the tenth plague: *vatechezaq Mitsrayim al-ha-am l'maher l'shalcham min ha-aretz* — *the Mitsrim were urgent upon the people, to send them out (l'shalcham) from the land in haste*. // **The verbal link is exact.** The Abram-expulsion of Gen 12:20 is verbally identical to the Israel-expulsion of Exod 12:33 — the same piel of *sh-l-ch*, the same context of Pharaonic dismissal under divine pressure, the same outcome of departure with great substance. The chapter verbally closes its proto-Exodus structure with the verb that will verbally open the Exodus's final scene. // The structural parallel discussed in scholarship (**Sarna, JPS, 1989**; **Garbini**, 1988; **Crüsemann**, 1992; the wider Pentateuchal-criticism literature) extends to the detail of *vay'shal'chu oto v'et-ishto v'et-kol-asher-lo* (sent him with his wife and all that he had), which parallels the Exodus departure-of-Israel-with-spoils. The chapter ends with Abram-and-household leaving Mitsrayim wealthier than they arrived, escorted out under royal command — the prefiguration of what Israel will do at the Exodus. // The WoH translation reads 'and they sent him away' matching ASV — preserves the verbal link forward to Exod 12:33. The claim_type is *inferred* because the proto-Exodus expulsion-verb reading is a *literary-critical inference* well-established in the consensus but synthetic across the two passages.

terms.literal: and the Canaanite and the Perizzite (were) then dwelling in the land
terms.standardEnglish: And the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then in the land
terms.wohChoice: And the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land
terms.rationale: **The chapter's most-cited Documentary-Hypothesis flag.** The temporal-perspective parenthetical at Gen 13:7 — *v-ha-Kna'ani v-ha-Prizi az yoshev ba-aretz* — uses the participial *yoshev* (dwelling) with the temporal adverb *az* (then) to foreground a redactional vantage *later than* the events being narrated. The parenthetical only does its work if it informs the audience of a state of affairs they would not have assumed by default — i.e., if the reader is positioned in a time when the Canaanite-Perizzite presence is no longer the dominant feature of the land. // **The verse pairs with Gen 12:6** — *v-ha-Kna'ani az ba-aretz* — which uses the singular Canaanite. The expanded inventory here at 13:7 doubles it with the Perizzite, anticipating the longer ethnographic lists at Gen 15:18-21 (ten peoples), Gen 34:30 (Canaanite + Perizzite again, in Jacob's protest after Dinah), and across Deuteronomy (Deut 7:1, 20:17) and Joshua (Josh 3:10, 24:11). The doubling has been read as either a redactional expansion of the 12:6 form (so Westermann, *Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia 1985, p. 176) or as a context-specific shift naming the two peoples Abram and Lot's herders would have been in direct competition with for grazing-rights in the hill country (so Wenham, *WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 297). // **The Documentary Hypothesis reading** treats both notices as J-stratum parentheticals from a post-conquest perspective (Wellhausen, *Prolegomena*, 1883; Gunkel, *Genesis*, 1901; von Rad, *Genesis*, 1972 ET; Speiser, *Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, p. 87). The reading is mainstream in critical scholarship but not universally accepted; Cassuto (*A Commentary on the Book of Genesis*, 1944 Hebrew / 1961 ET, vol. II, p. 326) and Kitchen (*On the Reliability of the Old Testament*, 2003) argue that the parentheticals are compatible with a pre-conquest narrator describing the ethnographic situation of the patriarchs' own day. // The WoH translation preserves the participial-progressive force ('were then dwelling') matching ASV's sense; the temporal *az* is kept as 'then'. The claim_type is *inferred* because the redactional-seam reading is a well-attested critical-scholarly inference, not an explicit statement in the text; the dating question remains open in scholarship.

terms.literal: like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim
terms.standardEnglish: like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt
terms.wohChoice: like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim
terms.rationale: **The doubled simile at Gen 13:10 is the chapter's most consequential inter-textual move.** Two of the Hebrew Bible's most freighted geographical referents are yoked to the pre-destruction plain of the Jordan. // **First, *gan YHWH*.** The phrase is a lexical back-reference to *gan b-Eden* of Gen 2:8 — the only other 'garden' associated with YHWH-Elohim in the primeval narratives. The Hebrew connects them directly: both are the *gan* of YHWH-Elohim, both are primeval-fertility prototypes, both are loci where the human / divine boundary is mediated through agricultural-spatial terms. The reading is mainstream: Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia 1985, p. 178), Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 100), Hamilton (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 393), Alter (*Five Books of Moses*, 2004, p. 65), and von Rad (*Genesis*, 1972 ET, p. 173) all flag the Eden allusion. The phrase frames the Jordan plain as a second Eden and sets up the narrative engine of the next several chapters: Lot has chosen the Eden-like region precisely when YHWH is about to destroy it. The narrator's parenthetical *lifnei shachet YHWH et-Sodom v-et-Amorah* ('before YHWH destroyed Sodom and Amorah') makes the typology explicit by name. // **The Eden-loss / Sodom-destruction parallel.** Genesis stages a clear type-scene pairing across chs 2-3 and chs 18-19: a fertile divinely-favored region; a human action that violates its terms (eating from the forbidden tree / Sodom's chata'im); expulsion-and-destruction (3:23-24 / 19:24-25); a flaming sword from the divine sphere (3:24) / fire-and-sulfur from the skies (19:24). The reading is critical-consensus and pre-dates the modern literary-critical turn. // **Second, *eretz Mitsrayim*.** The Nile-delta comparison stages a different resonance: the Jordan plain is a Nile-grade agricultural prototype, irrigated and produce-rich, the model the wilderness generation will remember nostalgically (Num 11:5, *we remember the fish we ate in Mitsrayim freely, the cucumbers and the melons and the leeks and the onions and the garlic*). Lot is choosing on Mitsrayim-grade criteria — the same evaluative frame that drew Abram down to Egypt in the famine of 12:10 — and the chapter is structurally rhyming Lot's choice with Abram's earlier descent. // **The Wheel of Heaven lens** notes the back-reference without arguing the full cosmology: Gen 1's primeval-creation account and Gen 2-3's Eden-garden account are taken as describing a single project-beginning context, and the *gan-YHWH* at 13:10 invites the reader to hold that context in mind while the next chapter sequence runs the destruction-and-restart pattern through Sodom-Amorah. The translation reads literally; the typological reading is held in commentary. // The WoH translation preserves the doubled simile literally ('like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim'), matching ASV. The claim_type is *direct* because the lexical back-reference is grammatically explicit in the Hebrew (*gan* of YHWH is the same construction as *gan b-Eden*) and the inter-textual reading is the scholarly consensus; what the back-reference *means* theologically remains open, but that it *is* a back-reference is uncontested.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal imperative) + politeness particle + noun (eyes) + 2ms suffix
terms.rationale: **The patriarchal seeing-formula.** The construction *nasa eynayim* — literally *to lift the eyes* — is the Hebrew Bible's standard idiom for raised-gaze perception, often introducing a visionary, revelatory, or recognitional moment. The verb *nasa* (n-s-' qal: lift, carry, bear up) governs the object *eynayim* (eyes); the imperative form *sa-na* (lift, please) adds the politeness / cohortative particle *-na*. The formula's inaugural occurrence in the patriarchal corpus is here at Gen 13:14, in YHWH's direct address to Abram after Lot's separation. // **The formula recurs across the patriarchal narratives:** Gen 18:2 (Abraham at Mamre sees the three visitors approaching); Gen 22:4 (Abraham on the third day of the *aqedah* journey sees the place of the binding from afar); Gen 22:13 (Abraham sees the ram caught in the thicket — the substitute); Gen 24:63 (Isaac at evening lifts his eyes and sees the approaching camels carrying Rebekah); Gen 24:64 (Rebekah parallel-action lifts her eyes and sees Isaac); Gen 31:10 (Jacob's dream-vision of the striped flocks: *v-esa eynai v-ere ba-chalom*); Gen 31:12 (the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* in Jacob's dream cites the same formula: *sa-na eynekha u-re'eh*); Gen 33:1 (Jacob lifts his eyes and sees Esau approaching with the four hundred men); Gen 33:5 (Jacob lifts his eyes and sees the women and children); Gen 37:25 (Joseph's brothers lift their eyes and see the Ishmaelite caravan); Gen 43:29 (Joseph lifts his eyes and sees Benjamin). The formula crosses the entire patriarchal arc as the standard threshold-of-vision marker. // **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia 1985, p. 180) notes the formulaic character; **Alter** (*Five Books of Moses*, 2004) flags the consistent use; **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 394) observes that in the patriarchal narratives the formula functions almost always to introduce a revelation or a recognition that changes the narrative direction. // The WoH translation preserves the verb-phrase 'lift up your eyes' across all occurrences, matching ASV; the politeness particle *-na* is rendered as 'now' (ASV convention) where it appears. The claim_type is *direct* because the formula is grammatically explicit in the Hebrew at every cited verse and the recurrence-set is an uncontested fact of the text.

terms.source: כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ / כַּכּוֹכָבִים / כַּחוֹל אֲשֶׁר עַל־שְׂפַת הַיָּם
terms.translit: ka-afar ha-aretz / ka-kokhavim / ka-chol asher al-s'fat ha-yam
terms.literal: as the dust of the earth / as the stars / as the sand on the lip of the sea
terms.standardEnglish: as the dust of the earth / as the stars of heaven / as the sand on the seashore
terms.wohChoice: as the dust of the earth / as the stars of the heavens / as the sand on the seashore
terms.rationale: **Three patriarchal seed-comparisons run as a deliberate set across the Abraham cycle.** The triad is: **dust of the earth** (Gen 13:16 — the chapter's promise-expansion after Lot's separation), **stars of the heavens** (Gen 15:5 — the night-vision count-the-stars promise), and **sand on the seashore** (Gen 22:17, paired with the stars in the *aqedah* covenant-confirmation). // **The cosmographic distribution is deliberate.** Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 100) and Alter (*Five Books of Moses*, 2004, p. 66) both treat the three as a set; Hamilton (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 396) and Wenham (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 298) note the same. The three comparisons cover the principal terrestrial registers: **dust** (earth, immanent, lowly — the *afar min ha-adamah* from which humanity was shaped at Gen 2:7 and to which it returns at 3:19); **stars** (sky, transcendent, height — the host of *kol-tz'va ha-shamayim* of Gen 2:1); **sand** (shore, the threshold between dry land and sea — the boundary that names the limits of the land). The triad reads as a deliberate distribution of the seed across earth, sky, and shore-margin. // **Hamilton specifically reads the dust-comparison at 13:16 as a creation-recall** — the patriarch's seed will share the substance of the original adamic forming-material. The reading sharpens the contrast with the more transcendent stars-image of 15:5: dust ties the promise to the ground and to the substance of the human; stars ties it to the heavens and to the divine perspective. // **The triad recurs in expanded form across the Hebrew Bible.** Stars-image: Gen 26:4 (to Isaac), Exod 32:13 (Moses's intercession), Deut 1:10, 10:22, 28:62, 1 Chr 27:23, Neh 9:23. Sand-image: Gen 32:13 (Jacob's prayer at the Jabbok), Josh 11:4, Judg 7:12, 1 Sam 13:5, 1 Kgs 4:20, Isa 10:22 (Romans 9:27 cites this), Jer 33:22, Hos 1:10. Dust-image: 2 Chr 1:9 (Solomon's prayer) explicitly reuses the dust comparison. The three together constitute the canonical Hebrew Bible vocabulary for the multiplied-seed promise. // The WoH translation preserves each comparison literally (*as the dust of the earth*, *as the stars*, *as the sand on the seashore*), matching ASV. The claim_type is *direct* because the three comparisons are grammatically explicit at their cited verses; calling them a triad is a scholarly observation but the observation is well-attested and uncontested.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal imperative) + verb (hithpael imperative) + prep + noun
terms.literal: arise, walk-thyself-about in the land, in the length of it and in the breadth of it
terms.standardEnglish: Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it
terms.wohChoice: Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it
terms.rationale: **The covenantal land-walking formula at Gen 13:17 names a juridical-symbolic act.** *Qum hit'hallekh ba-aretz l-orkah ul-rochbah* — the hithpael of h-l-k (walk-about, traverse-iteratively, walk-oneself) with *ba-aretz* (in the land) plus the *l-orkah ul-rochbah* doubling (in its length and in its breadth) — has been read across the modern critical tradition as a covenantal taking-possession by traversal. The patriarch's walking the boundaries of the land *is itself* a legal act of taking title to it. // **The reading goes back to David Daube, *Studies in Biblical Law* (1947), §3** — comparing the rite to the Roman *usucapio* (taking title by uninterrupted use) and to the Anglo-Saxon *seisin*-by-perambulation (the medieval legal act of taking possession of a granted estate by walking its boundaries). Daube's reading has been adopted by **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, p. 100), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 101), **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 181), and **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 299). The German biblical-law tradition discusses the same rite under the term *Begehungsritus* — boundary-walking ceremony. // **The parallel formulae make the reading load-bearing across the Hexateuch:** **Josh 1:3** — *kol-makom asher tidrokh kaf-raglekhem bo lakhem n'tatiyv* — *every place where the sole of your foot shall tread, to you have I given it* — uses the foot-treading possession-formula at the inauguration of the conquest. **Josh 18:8** — *qumu v-hit'halku ba-aretz v-kitvu otah* — *arise, walk through the land and describe it* — uses the same hithpael of h-l-k with *ba-aretz* in the seven-tribes-land-allotment context, making the walking-and-describing the explicit prerequisite for the casting of lots. **Deut 11:24** reiterates the foot-treading possession-formula in Moses's speech anticipating the conquest. // **Distinct from but related to** the broader hithpael-of-h-l-k uses: the Eden walking-formula of Gen 3:8 (*mit'hallekh b-gan*, YHWH-Elohim walking in the garden), and the patriarchal walking-with-Elohim formula of Gen 5:22 (Enoch), 6:9 (Noah), 17:1 (Abraham's *hit'hallekh l-fanai*), 24:40 (Abraham's servant's description), 48:15 (Jacob's blessing). Those uses name an ethical-relational walking-before-Elohim; the 13:17 use names a juridical-spatial walking-the-land. The two senses are related (the hithpael of iterative-reflexive walking covers both) but distinguishable. // The WoH translation preserves 'walk through the land', matching ASV; the doubled *l-orkah ul-rochbah* is kept as 'in the length of it and in the breadth of it'. The claim_type is *inferred* because the juridical-act framing is the modern scholarly synthesis (Daube 1947 and the consensus following him), not an explicit statement in the verse; what the verse explicitly commands is the walking, and the reading-as-possession-rite is the scholarly inference.

terms.standardEnglish: oaks of Mamre / terebinths of Mamre
terms.rationale: **The Mamre site near Hebron is the recurring locus of the Abraham cycle's southern hill-country residence and the geographical anchor of the patriarchal-matriarchal burial-narratives.** *Elonei Mamre* — אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא — is morphologically parallel to but distinct from *elon Moreh* (Gen 12:6): the plural construct *elonei* names a *grove* of trees rather than a single tree, and *Mamre* is a different proper name from *Moreh*. The two sacred-tree sites should not be conflated. // **The proper name *Mamre*.** Gen 14:13 and 14:24 explicitly name **Mamre the Amorite** (אִישׁ הָאֱמֹרִי, *ish ha-Emori*) as one of Abram's three Amorite allies in the war of the four kings — paired with his brothers Eshkol and Aner. The grove is therefore named for its human owner / occupant; the place-name is metonymic from the man-name. Gen 23:17, 19 specify that the field of Ephron the Hittite which Abraham purchased for Sarah's burial is *liphnei Mamre* (before / opposite Mamre); Gen 25:9 places Isaac and Ishmael burying Abraham at the same site; Gen 35:27 places Jacob's reunion with Isaac at Mamre; Gen 49:30 and 50:13 anchor the Machpelah burial of Jacob at the same locus. // **The two readings of the place-history**: (a) the **classical historical reading** (Sarna, *JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 102; Wenham, *WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 300) treats Mamre as the Amorite contemporary of Abram, with the place-name following from his presence; (b) the **alternative aetiological reading** (Westermann, *Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 184) treats *Mamre* as a pre-existing place-name secondarily personified into an Amorite character. The first reading is more straightforward; the second flags that the name does not have a clear Hebrew etymology. // **The recurrence-set across the Abraham cycle** makes the entry forward-loaded narratively: **Gen 13:18** (here — Abram's first residence at the site after the separation from Lot); **Gen 14:13, 14:24** (Mamre as ally in the war of the four kings; Abram dwelling *b-elonei Mamre*); **Gen 18:1** (*va-yera elav YHWH b-elonei Mamre* — the three-visitors theophany, the most embodied YHWH-appearance in the patriarchal arc); **Gen 23:17, 19** (the field of Ephron near Mamre purchased for Sarah's burial); **Gen 25:9** (Abraham buried in the cave of Machpelah, *el-sdeh Ephron... asher al-pnei Mamre*); **Gen 35:27** (Jacob returns to Isaac at *Mamre qiryat ha-arba hi Chevron*); **Gen 49:30** and **Gen 50:13** (the Machpelah-Ephron-Mamre burial-anchor invoked in the Jacob-Joseph cycle). // **The sacred-tree pattern.** Both *elon Moreh* (Shechem) and *elonei Mamre* (Hebron) fit the patriarchal-corpus sacred-tree pattern: trees at named sites that function as theophany-and-altar locations (cf. Gen 35:4 the Shechem oak, Gen 35:8 *Allon Bakhut* at Bethel, Josh 24:26 the Shechem sanctuary oak, Judg 6:11 the Ophrah oak, Judg 9:6, 37 the Shechem diviners' oak). The two sites are parallels in the pattern but distinct entries — the Moreh site names a tree by its oracle-function (*moreh* = teacher / oracle-giver), the Mamre site names a tree-grove by its human owner. // **On the botanical question** (oak vs. terebinth): BDB groups *elon* / *elah* / *alon* under 'oak / terebinth'; HALOT distinguishes them with less than full confidence; LXX renders δρῦς (oak); ASV and NRSV translate 'oaks', NJPS 'terebinths', NIV 'great trees'. The WoH translation follows ASV with 'oaks of Mamre', consistent with the 12:6 'oak of Moreh' rendering. The species precision is not load-bearing for the verse. // The claim_type is *direct* because *elonei Mamre* is a grammatically explicit proper-name phrase at every cited verse and the site-identification is uncontested in scholarship; the WoH lexical choice ('oaks') matches ASV / NRSV consensus.

terms.literal: the Refa'im ("weakened-ones / shades" or "heroes / mighty-ones") / the Zuzim / the Emim ("terrors")
terms.rationale: **The pre-Israelite peoples struck by Kedorlaomer at Gen 14:5 form one node in a corpus-wide giant-peoples chain.** *Refa'im* in Ashteroth-Karnaim, *Zuzim* in Ham, *Emim* in Shaveh-Kiryathaim — three Trans-Jordanian groups whose names recur with explicit giant-equation in Deuteronomy 2-3. The 14:5 list is itself bare narrative; the load-bearing material is the Deuteronomistic cross-reference. // **The Deuteronomic equations are grammatically explicit.** **Deut 2:10-11** — *ha-Emim l'fanim yashvu vah, am gadol v-rav va-ram ka-Anaqim; Refa'im yechashvu af-hem ka-Anaqim* — *the Emim formerly dwelt there, a great people and numerous and tall as the Anaqim; they too were counted as Refa'im like the Anaqim*. **Deut 2:20-21** — *eretz Refa'im techashev af-hi... v-ha-Amonim yiqr'u lahem Zamzumim; am gadol v-rav va-ram ka-Anaqim* — the Zuzim of Gen 14:5 are equated with the Ammonite-named *Zamzumim* and again *counted as Refa'im like the Anaqim*. **Deut 3:11** — *ki raq Og melekh ha-Bashan nish'ar mi-yeter ha-Refa'im; hineh arso eres barzel... tesha amot orkah v-arba amot rochbah* — *for only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of the Refa'im; his bedstead was an iron bedstead, nine cubits its length and four cubits its breadth* (roughly 13.5 ft x 6 ft on the standard Hebrew cubit). The Deuteronomist explicitly identifies the 14:5 peoples with the *Anaqim* and reads them as a giant-stature population. // **The *Anaqim* are themselves linked back to the antediluvian *Nephilim*.** **Num 13:33** — the spies' report on the inhabitants of Canaan: *v-sham ra'inu et-ha-Nefilim, b-nei Anaq min-ha-Nefilim; va-nehi v-eyneynu ka-chagavim* — *and there we saw the Nephilim, the sons of Anaq, from the Nephilim; and we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers*. The verse explicitly identifies the *bnei Anaq* — the Anaqim — with the *Nefilim* of Gen 6:1-4. The chain therefore runs: **Gen 6:1-4 Nefilim → Num 13:33 b-nei Anaq / Anaqim → Deut 2-3 Refa'im / Emim / Zamzumim / Og of Bashan → Gen 14:5 Refa'im / Zuzim / Emim**. The Deuteronomist's framing is that these were a pre-Israelite, giant-stature population displaced by the Trans-Jordanian peoples (Moab dispossessed the Emim, Ammon dispossessed the Zuzim/Zamzumim, Edom dispossessed the Horites), and the Israelite conquest finished the displacement with Og of Bashan as the last remnant. // **Mainstream scholarly treatment.** **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 195-196) treats the 14:5 peoples as a pre-Israelite-population list with the giant-traditions explicitly noted. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 105) treats the same. **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, pp. 311-312) collates the Deut 2-3 cross-references in detail. **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 403) traces the recurrence-chain through Num 13:33 to the Gen 6:4 Nefilim. The reading is not a fringe move; the giant-stature understanding of the Refa'im is the consensus of the modern critical commentaries. // **A second sense of *Refa'im* in the Hebrew Bible** designates the *shades* of the dead — the underworld inhabitants (Isa 14:9, 26:14, 26:19; Ps 88:11; Prov 2:18, 9:18, 21:16; Job 26:5). The two senses (pre-Israelite giants / shades-of-dead) are tracked as a single lexeme in BDB and HALOT, with proposals to unify them (the deceased mighty-ones; the heroes whose shades persist) or treat them as homographs from distinct roots. Greenfield (1973), L'Heureux (*Rank Among the Canaanite Gods*, 1979), and Pope (*Job*, AB, 1965) discuss the Ugaritic *rpʾum* (the divinized dead, the heroic ancestors at Ugarit) as the cognate background. The 14:5 use is the giant-peoples sense; the shades-sense is a separate philological track. // **Ashteroth-Karnaim** (the city of the Refa'im at 14:5) is a Trans-Jordanian double-named site (*Ashteroth* + *Karnaim*, the two-horned Astarte-image city), associated with the Astarte-cult; **Shaveh-Kiryathaim** is a Moabite-region site (cf. Num 32:37, Josh 13:19); **Ham** of the Zuzim is the obscure third site, possibly identical with the modern Ham of central Jordan. // **The WoH translation preserves the bare proper-name transcriptions** (*Refa'im*, *Zuzim*, *Emim*) without parenthetical 'giants' glosses, matching ASV's *Rephaim*, *Zuzim*, *Emim*. The lens-relevant material — the Deut 2-3 explicit giant-equations and the Num 13:33 Anaqim-Nefilim chain back to Gen 6:1-4 — lives in the per-verse commentary at 14:5 and in this glossary entry. The lens posture is that the chain exists and is mainstream-attested; what the chain *means* about pre-Flood populations is for downstream chapters and the wiki, not for the translated text. // The claim_type is *inferred* because the Deut 2-3 cross-references are grammatically explicit in *those* verses, but reading the 14:5 list as part of a giant-peoples chain requires the cross-reference work; the inference itself is straightforward and scholarly-consensus, but it is a synthesis across verses, not a reading directly from 14:5 alone.

terms.partOfSpeech: gentilic noun, masculine singular with article
terms.literal: the Hebrew (one) / the Eber-descendant / the one-from-beyond
terms.rationale: **The first occurrence of the gentilic *Ivri* in the Hebrew Bible is at Gen 14:13** — *Avram ha-Ivri*, Abram the Hebrew. The etymology of *Ivri* is a long-standing scholarly dispute with three readings circulating across the modern critical literature. The dispute itself is *direct* — well-attested and uncontested as a dispute — even though no single reading is settled. // **Reading 1: the eponymous-ancestor reading (Eber).** The Genesis editor's own genealogical apparatus supports the derivation from **Eber** (עֵבֶר), the great-grandson of Shem. **Gen 10:21** singles out Shem as *avi kol-bnei Eber* — *father of all the sons of Eber* — naming Eber before the rest of the genealogy is laid out (a structural prolepsis that flags Eber's centrality). **Gen 10:24-25** and **Gen 11:14-17** trace the line from Shem → Arpachshad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg → ... → Terah → Abram. On this reading, *Ivri* is the standard gentilic from the proper name *Eber* (with the regular qatil-pattern gentilic formation: *Eber* → *Ivri*, parallel to *Yisra'el* → *Yisra'eli*). The reading is the in-text reading: it is what the Genesis editor's genealogy implies. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 107) and **Cassuto** (*From Noah to Abraham*, 1964, p. 201) defend this reading. // **Reading 2: the *habiru / apiru* social-class reading.** The Akkadian *ḫabiru* (variant spellings *ʿapiru*, *ḫapiru*, *SA.GAZ* logographically) is attested across the 2nd-millennium Near Eastern documentary corpus — **Amarna letters** (especially EA 286-290, the Jerusalem letters of Abdi-Heba, which complain about *habiru* incursions); **Mari archives**; **Nuzi tablets**; **Hittite treaties** (including the standard divine-witness lists naming the *Hapiru*-gods); **Egyptian execration texts and New Kingdom administrative texts** (especially the *ʿapiru* working on the building of Pi-Ramses at the time of Ramesses II — the corpus that has drawn the heaviest Exodus-connection speculation). The *habiru* designate a marginal social class of stateless, dependent, often-displaced persons — sometimes brigands, sometimes mercenaries, sometimes hired labor, sometimes refugees. **Greenberg** (*The Hab/piru*, 1955), **Bottéro** (*Le problème des Habiru*, 1954), **Na'aman** (*Habiru and Hebrews: The Transfer of a Social Term to the Literary Sphere*, JNES 1986), **Rainey** (1995), and **Lemche** (*The Israelites in History and Tradition*, 1998) work the philological-historical case. The lexical connection between *habiru* and *Ivri* is plausible but contested; the consonants line up (ḫ-b-r ↔ ʿ-b-r, with the well-attested Northwest-Semitic ḫ → ʿ shift), and the social-class designation matches the patriarchal nomadic-stateless context. On this reading, *Avram ha-Ivri* would be a social designation (Abram the displaced-dependent / Abram the marginal-outsider) rather than an ethnic one. // **Reading 3: the *eber* (beyond / across) reading.** The third etymology derives *Ivri* from the noun *eber* (עֵבֶר, 'side / region beyond'), pointing to Abram's Trans-Euphrates origin. **Josh 24:2-3** explicitly names the patriarchal origin *b-ever ha-nahar* — *across the River* (the Euphrates) — and notes the family's pre-monotheistic Trans-Euphratine context. The **LXX** at Gen 14:13 reads περάτης (*peratēs*, 'one-from-across, one-from-the-other-side'), explicitly preserving the *eber* etymology. The Greek translators read *ha-Ivri* as *the one from across (the river)*. **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, pp. 102-103) and **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 195-196) discuss this reading. // **The three readings are not strictly incompatible.** Greenberg (1955) and Rainey (1995) propose that the Genesis editor may have inherited a social-class term (*habiru / apiru*) whose meaning shifted over time, was eponymized through the genealogical figure of *Eber*, and accommodated the *eber* / Trans-Euphrates geographical reading as a secondary etymology. The three readings stack chronologically: a 2nd-millennium social-class background → a Genesis-editor genealogical eponymization → a LXX-translator geographical reading. // **The corpus-distribution of *Ivri* is informative.** Within Genesis, the gentilic recurs in the **Joseph narrative** — Gen 39:14, 39:17 (Potiphar's wife: *ha-ish ha-Ivri*, *the Hebrew man*); Gen 40:15 (Joseph: *me-eretz ha-Ivrim*, *from the land of the Hebrews*); Gen 41:12 (the cupbearer: *na'ar Ivri*, *a Hebrew youth*); Gen 43:32 (the Egyptians' food-segregation, the only verse using *Ivrim* as a plural for the Israelite-people-in-Egypt). The distribution is striking: the term is used **by foreigners about Israelites, or by Israelites about themselves to foreigners**. The gentilic functions as an *outsider's designation* in the patriarchal corpus — which is consistent with the *habiru* social-class background. The same pattern persists into Exodus (1:15, 1:16, 1:19, 2:6, 2:7, 2:11, 2:13, 3:18, 5:3, 7:16, 9:1, 9:13, 10:3) — in nearly every instance, the term is used by Egyptians of Israelites or by Israelites to Pharaoh. // **The WoH translation renders 'the Hebrew', matching ASV / NRSV / NJPS consensus**, without preempting the etymological dispute in the translated text. The lens-relevant material (the *habiru / apiru* social-class background, the LXX *peratēs* reading, the Genesis-editor eponymization through Eber) is carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:13. // The claim_type is *direct* because the dispute itself is grammatically explicit (the three readings each have textual support — Eber in Gen 10-11, *eber* in Josh 24:2 and LXX, *habiru* in the Amarna corpus) and the scholarly state-of-the-question is well-attested; the entry does not commit to one reading.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine plural with 3ms suffix (hapax legomenon)
terms.literal: his trained-ones / his initiated-ones / his retainers
terms.rationale: **The noun *chanikh* (חָנִיךְ) occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible** — a hapax legomenon. The verbal root **ch-n-k** (חנך) is well-attested in the broader Hebrew Bible with the basic semantic range *dedicate / inaugurate / train-up*: **Prov 22:6** *chanokh la-na'ar al-pi darko* — *train up a child in the way he should go*; **Deut 20:5** the man who has built a house *v-lo chanakho* — *and has not dedicated it*; the noun **chanukkah** (חֲנֻכָּה, 'dedication') from the same root, used of the dedication of altars (Num 7:10-11, 7:84, 7:88), the dedication of the Second Temple (Ezra 6:16), the dedication of the rebuilt walls of Jerusalem (Neh 12:27), and post-biblically the rededication of the Temple under the Hasmoneans that gives the holiday Hanukkah its name. The verbal root therefore covers *dedicate* (cultic), *inaugurate* (architectural), and *train up* (pedagogical). // **The exact sense of the noun *chanikh* at Gen 14:14 is underdetermined.** Four readings circulate: (a) **'trained men / trained retainers'** — the household members trained-up by Abram for armed service; matches the *train-up* sense of ch-n-k and the immediate context (*y-lidei beito*, *born of his house*). ASV, NRSV, KJV, RSV all read 'trained men'. (b) **'initiates / dedicated-ones'** — household members ritually dedicated to Abram's house; matches the *dedicate* sense of ch-n-k and reads the term as a juridical-status designation rather than a military one. (c) **'retainers / liegemen'** — Albright (*The Archaeology of Palestine*, 1949) and **Lambdin** (1953) adduced the **Egyptian *ḥnk*** parallel: the Middle Kingdom Egyptian execration texts and administrative documents use *ḥnk* of *retainers / dependents / liegemen* of an Asiatic chieftain — the term designating armed household-bondsmen owed to a tribal-patriarchal figure. The Egyptian parallel is the strongest comparative anchor for the verse: it gives a 2nd-millennium West-Semitic-context attestation of a closely-similar lexeme used in a closely-similar social context. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 108), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 313), and **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 404) all cite the Egyptian *ḥnk* parallel; NJPS reads *retainers* on this basis; Alter (*The Five Books of Moses*, 2004, p. 67) also reads *retainers*. (d) **'armed household-dependents'** is the synthesis reading. // **The number 318 has attracted a parallel post-biblical interpretive tradition: gematria.** The Talmudic homily at **b. Nedarim 32a** (also **b. Bava Batra 16a**) reads the 318 as the numerical value of **Eliezer** (אֱלִיעֶזֶר): aleph(1) + lamed(30) + yod(10) + ayin(70) + zayin(7) + resh(200) = 318. The reading equates the 318 with Abraham's chief servant Eliezer (named at Gen 15:2 as *Eliezer of Damascus*), reading the verse as 'he led forth Eliezer' rather than 'his 318 trained men'. The reading is post-biblical exegesis — the gematria-tradition develops in the Tannaitic and Amoraic periods — and is not the literal-historical reading. The reading is included here as **DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION** material: the rabbinic tradition's reading of the verse, not what the Hebrew literally says. **Gen Rab 43:2** (Bereshit Rabbah) attests the same gematria-reading. // **The literal-historical reading is the plain-sense 'his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen'** — a household militia drawn from Abram's domestically-born retainers (the *y-lidei beito*, 'born-of-his-house' construction, distinguishes them from purchased slaves; cf. Gen 17:12-13, 17:23, 17:27 for the formal distinction). The number 318 is large enough to constitute a respectable raiding party but small enough to be plausibly a single patriarch's retinue; the symbolic-numerical readings (gematria-Eliezer, the 318-as-the-numerical-value-of-various-things) are secondary. // **The WoH translation renders 'his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen'**, matching ASV. The hapax status, the ch-n-k root semantics, the Egyptian *ḥnk* parallel, and the rabbinic gematria-Eliezer tradition are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:14. // The claim_type is *inferred* because the lexical sense of the hapax cannot be settled from internal evidence alone — the root sense of ch-n-k (dedicate / train-up) and the Egyptian *ḥnk* parallel together give a defensible synthesis (*trained armed retainers*), but the precise sense is a scholarly inference, not a grammatically explicit datum.

terms.literal: my king is Tzedek / king of righteousness
terms.rationale: **The name *Malki-Tzedek* is morphologically a theophoric compound** — *malki* (1cs-suffixed construct: 'my king is...' / 'king of...') + *Tzedek* — and admits two principal compositional readings. The two readings are both pre-modern attested; the theophoric reading is the consensus among modern Semitists. // **Reading 1: the theophoric-name reading ('my king is Tzedek').** *Tzedek* is attested as a **West-Semitic divine name** at several anchor points: (a) **Ugaritic** — the deity-pair *Ṣdq* and *Mšr* (Tzedek and Mishor, *righteousness* and *uprightness*) attested in the divine-list KTU 1.123 and other Ugaritic texts as members of the divine assembly; (b) **Phoenician** — the historian Philo of Byblos (*Phoenician History*, preserved in Eusebius *Praeparatio Evangelica* 1.10), citing the ostensibly Phoenician priest Sanchuniathon, names *Sydyk* (Tzedek) as the father of the Dioscuri / Cabiri; (c) **Pre-Israelite Jerusalem** — the Jebusite king-name **Adoni-Tzedek** (אֲדֹנִי־צֶדֶק, 'my lord is Tzedek') at **Josh 10:1, 10:3**, ruling in Jerusalem at the time of the Israelite conquest. The pattern (theophoric Jerusalem-king-names compounded with *Tzedek*) is well-attested: Melchizedek at the patriarchal stratum, Adoni-Tzedek at the conquest stratum. The pair suggests that *Tzedek* was the local Jerusalem high-god name (or one of them) during the pre-Israelite period. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 203-205), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 109), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 316), **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 409), and **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, pp. 207-209) all read the name theophorically. // **Reading 2: the honorific-epithet reading ('king of righteousness').** **Hebrews 7:2** explicitly translates the name as βασιλεὺς δικαιοσύνης — *king of righteousness* — reading *malki-tzedek* as a construct compound 'king-of-righteousness' rather than as a theophoric. The reading is attested in the rabbinic tradition (with variations) and dominated the Christian and traditional Jewish reception. The grammatical objection is that the form *malki-* (with the *-i* suffix) is more naturally read as the 1cs-suffixed *my king* than as a construct chain (which would more typically be *melekh tzedek*, parallel to *melekh Shalem* in the same verse); but the construct-with-*-i*-ending is attested in proper names (the *-i* read as the so-called *hireq compaginis*, an archaic case-ending preserved in names). // **The figure of Melchizedek occurs only twice in the Hebrew Bible:** **Gen 14:18-20** (the present chapter) and **Ps 110:4** — *attah kohen l'olam al-divrati Malki-Tzedek* — *you are a priest forever after the order / manner of Melchizedek* — the royal-psalm verse that has carried the entire downstream priesthood-tradition. The Ps 110 verse is *direct* about the figure (it names him) and *direct* about his priestly office (it places the royal addressee in his priestly lineage) but does *not* unpack the name's etymology. // **DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION (flagged, not preempted):** (1) **Qumran 11QMelchizedek (11Q13)**, dated ~mid-1st-century BCE, makes Melchizedek a heavenly *Elohim*-figure / divine-council member executing eschatological judgment. The scroll explicitly applies Ps 82 (*Elohim stands in the divine assembly, among the elohim he gives judgment*) to Melchizedek as the judging Elohim, and reads the Isaiah 61 'year of jubilee / release' as the eschatological release Melchizedek will execute. The Melchizedek of 11Q13 is a quasi-angelic redeemer-judge — a heavenly figure, not a Canaanite king of Salem. The scroll is one of the most theologically significant Qumran texts and is the principal Jewish-tradition source for a transcendent reading of Melchizedek. **Horton** (*The Melchizedek Tradition*, 1976), **Kobelski** (*Melchizedek and Melchireša'*, 1981), and **Mason** (*You Are a Priest Forever*, 2008) treat 11Q13 in detail. (2) **Christian Hebrews 5-7 reception** — especially **Heb 7:1-17** — builds an entire christology of pre-Aaronic, pre-Levitical, eternal priesthood: Jesus as priest *kata tēn taxin Melchisedek*, 'after the order of Melchizedek', drawing on Ps 110:4. **Heb 7:3** famously characterizes the Genesis Melchizedek as *apatōr, amētōr, agenealogētos* — *without father, without mother, without genealogy* — reading the silence of Gen 14 about Melchizedek's lineage as a typological warrant for the eternal-priesthood christological move. (3) **Rabbinic traditions** variously identify Melchizedek with **Shem** son of Noah (**b. Nedarim 32b**, **Targum Yerushalmi on Gen 14:18**, **Pirqe d'Rabbi Eliezer**) — the move solves the question of how a pre-Aaronic priest could legitimately exist by routing him through the Noahic genealogy. // **All three reception streams post-date the Hebrew text by centuries.** The Translator and Editor explicitly do *not* preempt them in the translated English or in the glossary's *wohChoice*; they live in the per-verse commentary at 14:18 as flagged-not-endorsed reception material. // **The WoH translation renders 'Melchizedek'** as the standard transliteration, matching ASV / NRSV / NJPS consensus. The theophoric-name reading and the downstream reception streams are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary. // The claim_type is *direct* because the name *Malki-Tzedek* is grammatically explicit at Gen 14:18 and Ps 110:4, and the West-Semitic theophoric-compound reading is the scholarly consensus (Westermann, Sarna, Wenham, Hamilton, Cross); the entry does not commit to either the theophoric or the honorific etymological reading, but the name's two-element compound structure is itself direct.

**The place-name *Shalem* (שָׁלֵם) at Gen 14:18 has been identified with Jerusalem in the dominant canonical tradition.** The identification is direct in the sense that it is explicitly attested in the Hebrew Bible itself and in the earliest interpretive traditions; the philological case is not fully closed (a minority modern view treats *Shalem* as an independent site), but the canonical reading is consensus. // **The principal anchor for the Jerusalem identification is Psalm 76:3** (Heb 76:3, Eng 76:2) — *va-yhi b-Shalem suko u-m'onato b-Tziyon* — *his shelter was in Shalem, and his dwelling-place in Zion*. The parallelism explicitly equates **Shalem** with **Zion / Jerusalem** in a deliberately structured bi-cola verse. The Psalter is itself the canonical anchor: the Hebrew Bible internally identifies *Shalem* with Jerusalem. // **The pre-Israelite Jerusalem name-history.** The city's pre-Israelite name *(U)ru-šalim* / *Yerushalayim* is attested in the **Egyptian Execration Texts** (19th-18th century BCE: *Rušalimum*), in the **Amarna letters** (14th century BCE: *Urusalim*, in the letters of Abdi-Heba, EA 285-290), and in the **Assyrian annals** (Sennacherib, 7th century BCE: *Ursalimmu*). The name has been read as a theophoric compound (*ʾUru* 'foundation / city' + *Shalim*, the West-Semitic deity-name attested as the evening / dusk-god paired with **Shahar** the dawn-god in the Ugaritic *Shaḥar and Shalim* text KTU 1.23). On this analysis, the city's full name preserves the *Shalim* deity-element; the short form *Shalem* at Gen 14:18 could be read as preserving the pre-theophoric root. The relevance to the place-identification is that the city's pre-Israelite identity is well-attested and the *Shalem*-element of the name is grammatically and historically real. // **Qumran 1QapGen Genesis Apocryphon, column 22 (ca. mid-1st-century BCE)** explicitly identifies the *Shalem* of Gen 14:18 with Jerusalem: the scroll's paraphrase reads *l'Shalem hi Yerushalem* — *to Shalem which is Jerusalem* — making the identification explicit in the earliest extant reception. **Josephus** (*Antiquities* 1.180) and **Targum Onkelos** at Gen 14:18 also identify Shalem with Jerusalem. **LXX** at Gen 14:18 renders Σαλημ, the standard Greek transcription, with no explicit gloss — the identification is assumed. **Hebrews 7:1-2** identifies Salem with Jerusalem implicitly: the *king of peace* (βασιλεὺς εἰρήνης) etymology in Heb 7:2 reads *Shalem* through Hebrew *shalom* (peace), an etymological move that presupposes the place is identifiable. // **Minority alternative readings.** Two have been proposed: (a) The **independent-site reading** treats *Shalem* as a separate Trans-Jordan or Samaria-region town. **Gen 33:18** reads *va-yavo Ya'aqov shalem ir Shekhem* — which **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, p. 263) and others have read as *Jacob came safely [as adverbial] to the city of Shechem* rather than *Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem*. If a separate *Shalem* town existed near Shechem, the Gen 14:18 *Shalem* could conceivably be the same site; the **Salim** near **Aenon** of John 3:23 has also been proposed. The reading is minority and faces the Ps 76:3 parallelism directly. (b) The **divine-epithet reading** points to the Ugaritic *Shalim* deity (the evening / dusk-god paired with *Shahar* in KTU 1.23) and proposes that the original sense of *melekh Shalem* was '[priest-]king of [the deity] Shalem' — a theophoric construction rather than a place-name. The reading is plausible philologically (especially given the *Yerushalayim* etymology preserves *Shalim* as a theophoric element) but the contextual Hebrew flow (the parallel *melekh Sodom*, *king of Sodom*, in the same chapter strongly favors place-name reading) and the canonical Ps 76:3 identification are decisive against the reading as the primary sense at Gen 14:18. // **Mainstream scholarly treatment.** **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 203-204), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 109), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, pp. 316-317), and **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, pp. 408-409) all treat the Jerusalem identification as the canonical and most-likely reading, while noting the philological complications and the pre-Israelite *Shalim*-deity background. // **The WoH translation renders 'king of Shalem'** as a bare transliteration of the Hebrew place-name (rather than the English-conventional 'king of Salem', which is a Vulgate-routed transliteration via Latin); this matches the ASV / NRSV / NJPS practice of preserving Hebrew place-name spellings (with Salem as the more traditional English-conventional form). The canonical Jerusalem identification, the pre-Israelite name-history, and the philological alternatives are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:18. // The claim_type is *direct* because the *Shalem* place-name is grammatically explicit at Gen 14:18 and Ps 76:3, and the Jerusalem identification is explicitly attested in the Hebrew Bible itself (Ps 76:3 parallelism) and in the earliest interpretive traditions (1QapGen, LXX, Josephus, Targum Onkelos, Hebrews); the philological alternatives are minority readings within a directly-attested canonical identification.

**Gen 14:18 is the first occurrence of *kohen* (priest) in the Hebrew Bible.** The first-occurrence locus is theologically and structurally load-bearing for the entire downstream priesthood-tradition. // **The inaugural priest is non-Israelite, pre-Aaronic, pre-Sinaitic, and king-priest in a single office.** Three structural facts: (a) **Non-Israelite** — Melchizedek is a Canaanite king (of Shalem / Jerusalem) and not from any of the Hebrew patriarchal lines. The Hebrew Bible's first *kohen* is therefore a *foreign* priest. (b) **Pre-Aaronic and pre-Sinaitic** — the Aaronic priesthood is not established until **Exod 28:1, 29:1-37, Lev 8** at Sinai. Melchizedek's priesthood at Gen 14 precedes the Aaronic order by the entire Genesis-Exodus narrative arc. (c) **King-priest in a single office** — Melchizedek is *melekh Shalem* and *kohen l-El Elyon* in the same verse, combining royal and priestly office in a single figure. The combined office is the distinctive structural feature: the Aaronic order separates priesthood from kingship (the Levites are not kings); the only post-Aaronic figures who legitimately combine the two offices in the Hebrew Bible's idealized order are the Hasmoneans (de facto, contested) and the eschatological figures of Zechariah 6:9-15 (the *tsemach*) and Ps 110 (the royal addressee who is also *kohen l'olam al-divrati Malki-Tzedek*). // **The collocation *kohen l-El Elyon* (not *l-YHWH*) is significant.** Melchizedek is priest of *El Elyon* — the West-Semitic high-god name with the *Elyon* (Most High) epithet (see the *el-elyon* glossary entry). The verse does *not* say Melchizedek is priest of YHWH. The harmonization of *El Elyon* with YHWH is made by **Abram** at **Gen 14:22** — *YHWH El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz* — in the appositional oath-formula. The Genesis editor's move is to identify the *El Elyon* of the Melchizedek tradition with the *YHWH* of the Abrahamic tradition; but the editor does not put YHWH-priest language in Melchizedek's mouth or in the narrator's description of him. The pre-Israelite character of the Melchizedek tradition is preserved by the *El Elyon*-only language at vv 18-20. // **The lexeme *kohen* in the broader Semitic context.** The Hebrew *kohen* has cognates across West-Semitic: **Phoenician *khn*** (attested as a priestly title in the Karatepe inscription and elsewhere); **Aramaic *kahna*** (the standard Aramaic word for *priest*, used in the Targums and in Daniel 5:4); **Arabic *kāhin*** (with semantic specialization to *seer / soothsayer / diviner* rather than officiating priest, but cognate). The wide West-Semitic distribution means the Hebrew Bible's first *kohen* is using a culturally-shared cultic vocabulary; Melchizedek's priesthood is intelligible in the West-Semitic religious context, not a distinctively Israelite institution. // **The corpus-distribution of *kohen* in the Hebrew Bible** is wide: ~750 occurrences in the MT. The lexeme covers Aaronic priests (the dominant use), Levitical priests (in some traditions distinguished from Aaronic, in others equated), non-Israelite priests (Potiphera priest of On at Gen 41:45, 50; 46:20; Jethro priest of Midian at Exod 2:16, 3:1, 18:1; Amaziah priest of Bethel at Amos 7:10-17; the priests of Baal and Dagon and other foreign deities), royal officials in pre-monarchic and monarchic Israel (the Davidic *kohanim* of 2 Sam 8:18 are problematic — David's sons as *kohanim*? — and the parallel 1 Chr 18:17 changes the term to *ha-rishonim*, the chief officials, signaling redactional discomfort with the original term), and the eschatological / messianic priestly figures of Ps 110:4, Zech 6:9-15, Mal 2:7. // **DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION (flagged, not preempted).** The pre-Aaronic, pre-Sinaitic, king-priest character of the Melchizedek priesthood becomes the load-bearing fact of **Hebrews 5-7** — especially **Heb 7:1-17**, which builds an entire christological priesthood-argument on the priority of Melchizedek over Aaron. **Heb 7:11** — *if perfection were through the Levitical priesthood... what further need would there have been for another priest to arise after the order of Melchizedek?* — uses the pre-Aaronic locus to subordinate the Levitical order to the Melchizedek order. The reading is post-biblical Christian exegesis and is not the Hebrew Bible's reading of itself, but the structural fact it leans on — that Gen 14:18 is the first *kohen* and pre-dates Sinai — is direct. // **The WoH translation renders 'priest of El Elyon'**, matching ASV / NRSV / NJPS consensus on *kohen* as *priest*. The first-occurrence status, the pre-Aaronic / pre-Sinaitic character, the king-priest combined office, the *kohen l-El Elyon* not *l-YHWH* collocation, and the downstream Hebrews 5-7 reception are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:18. // The claim_type is *direct* because all the structural facts — the first-occurrence status, the pre-Aaronic locus, the *l-El Elyon* collocation, the combined king-priest office — are grammatically explicit at Gen 14:18 and verifiable by lexical search of the Hebrew Bible.

**El Elyon is the divine name introduced into the Hebrew Bible at Gen 14:18-22** — the chapter's keystone lexical site, and one of the most discussed divine-name compounds in the West-Semitic and Hebrew Bible material. The compound joins two distinct elements, each with significant West-Semitic background. // **Element 1: *El* (אֵל).** The shared West-Semitic high-god name. Attested across the West-Semitic family: **Ugaritic *ʾIl*** — the chief of the divine assembly, the *bull-El* (*ṯr ʾil*), the *father of years* (*ʾab šnm*), creator and high-god of the Canaanite pantheon (Cross, *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, pp. 13-75; Smith, *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001, pp. 41-66); **Phoenician *ʾl*** (the Karatepe inscription, Eshmunazar, the Tabnit inscriptions); **Akkadian *ilu*** as the generic god-word and as a personal divine name; **Aramaic *ʾl*** at Sefire and elsewhere. In the Hebrew Bible, *El* functions both as a generic god-word (often paralleled with *Elohim*) and as the divine-name component in theophoric compounds: *El Shaddai* (Gen 17:1, 28:3, 35:11, 48:3, Exod 6:3), *El Olam* (Gen 21:33), *El Bethel* (Gen 31:13, 35:7), *El Elohei Yisra'el* (Gen 33:20). The patriarchal narratives' systematic use of *El*-compounds has been read since **Albrecht Alt's *The God of the Fathers* (1929)** and **Frank Moore Cross's *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* (1973)** as preserving an earlier stratum of patriarchal religion in which the patriarchs worshipped *El* at named sanctuary-sites — *El Shaddai* at one sanctuary, *El Bethel* at another, *El Elyon* at Jerusalem-Shalem — and the *YHWH*-name harmonization came later (with the editor's appositional construction at v 22 being the explicit harmonizing move). The reading is mainstream modern critical scholarship. // **Element 2: *Elyon* (עֶלְיוֹן).** The epithet *highest / uppermost / most-high*, derived from the root *ʿ-l-h* (*go up*). *Elyon* is itself attested as an **independent divine name** in West-Semitic: **Philo of Byblos** (preserved in Eusebius, *Praeparatio Evangelica* 1.10), citing the (purportedly) Phoenician priest **Sanchuniathon**, names **Elioun** (Ἐλιοῦν), called *Hypsistos* (Most High), as a distinct deity — the father of *Ouranos* and *Ge* (Sky and Earth) in the Phoenician theogony. The attestation is late (Philo writes ca. 1st-2nd century CE, citing material he claims is far older), but it preserves a memory of *Elyon* as an independent divine name in the West-Semitic pantheon. **Within the Hebrew Bible**, *Elyon* occurs alone (not compounded with *El*) at several anchor points: **Num 24:16** — Balaam's third oracle, *u-yodea da'at Elyon* — *and one who knows the knowledge of Elyon*; **Deut 32:8** — the load-bearing divine-council verse (see below); **Ps 9:3, 7:18, 21:8, 46:5, 47:3, 50:14, 57:3, 73:11, 77:11, 78:17, 78:35, 78:56, 82:6, 83:19, 91:1, 91:9, 92:2, 97:9, 107:11, Isa 14:14, Lam 3:35, 3:38** — the Psalter uses *Elyon* widely as YHWH's epithet, often in royal-psalm and praise contexts. // **Deuteronomy 32:8-9 is the most lens-relevant locus** for the *Elyon* divine-council material. The verse: *b-hanchel Elyon goyim b-haphrido bnei adam, yatzev g'vulot amim l-mispar bnei [Elohim / Yisra'el]* — *when Elyon allotted the nations as their inheritance, when he separated the sons of man, he established the borders of the peoples according to the number of the [sons of Elohim / sons of Israel]*. **Textual variant: MT reads *bnei Yisra'el* (sons of Israel); the DSS reading at 4QDeut^j and 4QDeut^q reads *bnei Elohim* (sons of Elohim) — divine sons / divine beings; the LXX reads *aggelōn theou* (angels of God), itself a translation of an underlying *bnei Elohim* / *bnei El*.** The DSS / LXX reading is the modern scholarly consensus as the original reading: **Skehan** (*A Fragment of the 'Song of Moses' (Deut. 32) from Qumran*, BASOR 1959), **Tov** (*Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible*, 3rd ed. 2012), **Tigay** (*JPS Deuteronomy*, 1996, Excursus 31 on Deut 32:8), and the **NRSV** (which translates from the DSS / LXX reading: 'according to the number of the gods') and **NJPS** all accept the DSS reading. The reading recovers a divine-council picture: *Elyon* is the high-god who, at the time of the post-Babel scattering of the nations, allots the peoples to the *bnei Elohim* — the divine sons, the divine council — with each people receiving a divine patron. **Verse 9** continues: *ki chelek YHWH amo, Ya'akov chevel nachalato* — *for YHWH's portion is his people, Jacob is the lot of his inheritance*. **The two verses together place Elyon and YHWH in a hierarchical-distinct relation in one stratum of the corpus**: *Elyon* is the allocating high-god; *YHWH* is the divine patron who receives Jacob/Israel as his allotment. The reading is the canonical critical reading of Deut 32:8-9. // **The Gen 14 editor's harmonization at v 22.** Abram swears by *YHWH El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz* — explicitly identifying YHWH *with* the El Elyon of Melchizedek's blessing at vv 18-20. The appositional construction is the harmonizing-syncretism move: the Genesis editor accepts the Melchizedek-tradition's El Elyon as the name of the high-god, but identifies that high-god with YHWH. The move is precisely parallel to the Documentary-Hypothesis reading of Exod 6:3 — *I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them* — where the editor harmonizes the patriarchal *El Shaddai*-religion with the Mosaic YHWH-name. **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, pp. 50-60), **Smith** (*The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001, pp. 135-148), **Mullen** (*The Divine Council in Canaanite and Early Hebrew Literature*, 1980) treat the harmonization-pattern in detail. // **The lens-relevant material — the West-Semitic-pantheon framework, the Deut 32:8 divine-council reading, the El-and-YHWH harmonization** — is **mainstream modern critical scholarship**, not a Wheel-of-Heaven-distinctive claim. The WoH lens reads through this scholarly framework but does not need to extend it; the framework already supplies the layered-divine-council picture the lens is interested in. The Editor's posture on this entry is therefore *measured*: the entry reports what the mainstream scholarship reports, with the Deut 32:8 DSS-versus-MT textual variant flagged accurately and the West-Semitic *El* and *Elyon* attestations cited with the standard scholarly sources. The WoH lens-specific reading lives elsewhere (in the wiki, in the chapter-essay layer of the corpus, in the methodology page) — not in this glossary entry. // **The WoH translation renders 'El Elyon'** as a bare transliteration of the divine-name compound (rather than the conventional 'God Most High'), per the WoH convention of preserving Hebrew divine-name compounds in translation. The convention preserves the West-Semitic-pantheon character of the name and refuses the silent-singularization that the English 'God Most High' performs. // The claim_type is *direct* on the name itself (the compound *El Elyon* is grammatically explicit at Gen 14:18, 14:19, 14:20, 14:22; the West-Semitic *El* and *Elyon* attestations are direct documentary evidence; the Deut 32:8 DSS *bnei Elohim* reading is direct textual data). The West-Semitic-pantheon framework reading is *scholarly synthesis* (Cross 1973, Smith 2001) but the synthesis is mainstream modern critical scholarship — direct in the sense that the field treats it as the consensus reconstruction, not as a fringe inference.

terms.literal: acquirer / creator of heaven and earth
terms.standardEnglish: possessor of heaven and earth / maker of heaven and earth
terms.rationale:
**The epithet *koneh shamayim va-aretz* at Gen 14:19 and 14:22 is an inherited West-Semitic high-god title** — attached in the Hebrew Bible to *El Elyon* — and one of the most directly attested cross-Semitic philological links in the canon.

The Hebrew verb-root *q-n-h* has a lexical duality: in dominant Biblical Hebrew usage it means *acquire / possess / buy* (Gen 4:1, 25:10, Exod 21:2, and the noun *miqneh*); in an older, more poetic register it means *create / produce / beget*. The create-sense is preserved at **Deut 32:6** *halo hu avikha qanekha* ('is he not your father who created you'), at **Ps 139:13** *ki atah qanita khilyotai* ('for you created my inward parts' — a womb-formation context), and at **Prov 8:22** *YHWH qanani reshit darko* (the personified Wisdom's origin — and the verse over which the entire 4th-century Arian-Athanasian Christological dispute turned).

The **cross-Semitic parallels are decisive for the *create*-sense in divine-epithet contexts**. The Ugaritic high-god **El** bears the epithet ***qny ʾrṣ*** — *creator of the earth*. The Phoenician **Karatepe inscription** (KAI 26, 8th c. BCE) contains the divine-name compound ***ʾl qn ʾrṣ*** — *El, creator of the earth* — the closest comparative cognate to the Hebrew Gen 14 formula (identical form, same divine-name pairing). The Hittite-Hurrian **Elkunirsa myth** preserves the same divine epithet as a personal name (*Elkunirsa* = ʾEl-qōnē-ʾaraṣ). The 2nd-millennium West-Semitic *qny ʾrṣ* epithet is well-attested as a stable, transmissible high-god title attached to *El*, and in every attested cross-Semitic instance the meaning is unambiguously *create*, not *possess*.

Westermann (1985, p. 204), Sarna (1989, p. 109), Wenham (1987, p. 317), Hamilton (1990, p. 410), Cross (1973, pp. 15-18, 50-52), and Smith (*The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001) all treat the epithet as inherited West-Semitic. The modern English translation tradition splits on which Hebrew sense — *possess* or *create* — best preserves the inherited meaning: ASV / KJV / RSV read 'possessor'; NRSV / NJPS / NIV / Alter / Speiser read 'maker / creator'.

The WoH translation reads **'creator of heaven and earth'**, departing from ASV in favor of the modern critical consensus. The choice rests on the cross-Semitic epithet evidence: in the specific *El qn ʾrṣ* high-god-epithet context that Gen 14:19, 22 inherits, the West-Semitic comparators preserve only the *create* sense. The internal Hebrew create-sense attestations (Deut 32:6, Ps 139:13, Prov 8:22) reinforce that the verb's create-sense is alive in early Hebrew divine-context usage. The dominant Biblical Hebrew *possess*-sense of *q-n-h* is preserved in the glossary apparatus for the lexical layer; the wohChoice 'creator' preserves the inherited West-Semitic epithet meaning in the translation.

**Gen 14:20 is the first occurrence of *ma'aser* (tithe) in the Hebrew Bible.** *Va-yiten lo ma'aser mi-kol* — *and he gave him a tithe of all*. The verse establishes the institution of the tithe at a pre-Sinai, pre-Mosaic, pre-Aaronic locus: Abram gives a tenth of the spoils to a non-Israelite priest of El Elyon. The first-occurrence locus is structurally and theologically load-bearing. // **The grammatical ambiguity of *va-yiten lo*.** The Hebrew is ambiguous about the direction of the giving: *va-yiten lo* — *and he gave to him* — does not specify who gave to whom. Reading 1: **Abram gave the tithe to Melchizedek** (the consensus reading, supported by the parallel structure of the previous verses: Melchizedek blesses Abram in vv 19-20a; Abram responds with a tithe in v 20b — the standard ancient Near Eastern priest-king/recipient pattern); the reading is what **Hebrews 7:2** explicitly assumes (*and to him Abraham apportioned a tenth of everything*) and what the patristic, rabbinic, and modern critical traditions take. Reading 2: **Melchizedek gave the tithe to Abram** (a minority reading; would imply Melchizedek shared a tenth of the priestly stores with Abram; the reading is grammatically possible but contextually strained). The WoH translation follows the consensus Reading 1: 'and he [Abram] gave him [Melchizedek] a tithe of all'. // **The pre-Sinai, pre-Aaronic institution.** Gen 14:20 establishes the tithe as a Hebrew Bible institution before the Sinai legislation. The Aaronic / Levitical tithe-legislation is not given until **Lev 27:30-33** (the holiness-code tithe-law: *every tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the tree, is YHWH's*) and **Num 18:21-32** (the Levitical tithe: the Levites receive *kol-ma'aser b-Yisra'el* — *every tithe in Israel* — as their inheritance in lieu of land). The Deuteronomic tithe-laws (**Deut 12:6, 11, 17**; **Deut 14:22-29** — the *ma'aser sheni*, the second tithe; **Deut 26:12** — the triennial tithe for the Levite, sojourner, orphan, and widow) further develop the institution. The full corpus of biblical tithe-law builds out from Sinai; Gen 14:20 stands as the *aetiological precedent*: the patriarch's tithe to the El-Elyon-priest pre-figures and warrants the later institution. // **Gen 28:22 — Jacob's tithe at Bethel** is the second pre-Sinai tithe-instance and the structural parallel to Gen 14:20. After the Bethel theophany, Jacob vows: *v-kol asher titen-li aser a-asrenu lakh* — *and of all that you give me, I will surely give the tenth to you*. The two pre-Sinai tithe-instances — Abram at Gen 14:20 (to the Melchizedek-Elyon priest) and Jacob at Gen 28:22 (to YHWH at Bethel) — together establish the patriarchal-stratum tithe institution. The rabbinic tradition (Tg. Yer. on Gen 14:20; Tg. PsJ on Gen 28:22) and the modern critical commentaries (Sarna, *JPS Genesis*, 1989, pp. 109, 200; Wenham, *WBC Genesis*, 1987, pp. 317-318) treat the two verses as a deliberate set. // **Cross-cultural tithe-parallels.** The ancient Near Eastern tithe institution is well-attested: **Mesopotamian *ešrû / ešretu*** (tithe) — Sumerian and Akkadian temple-economy tithes from the third millennium onward; **Ugaritic *mʿšr*** — the cognate noun, attested in the Ugaritic economic and administrative texts; **Egyptian temple-tithes** — the New Kingdom temple economy depended heavily on tithe-like state revenues. **Phoenician** evidence (the Carthaginian tariff inscriptions, KAI 69 and the Marseilles Tariff) attests detailed sacrificial-tithe regulations cognate to the Levitical material. The Genesis tithe-institution is intelligible in the broader West-Semitic / ANE temple-economy context; the patriarchal narratives' use of the institution is consistent with the 2nd-millennium West-Semitic background. // **DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION (flagged, not preempted): Hebrews 7:1-10.** The pre-Aaronic locus of Gen 14:20 becomes the load-bearing fact of an entire Christological priesthood-argument. **Heb 7:2-10** — *to him Abraham apportioned a tenth of everything... see how great this man was to whom even the patriarch Abraham gave a tithe of the spoils!... and one might even say that Levi himself, who receives tithes, paid tithes through Abraham, for he was still in the loins of his ancestor when Melchizedek met him* — uses the Genesis tithe-instance to argue the superiority of the Melchizedek priesthood over the Aaronic / Levitical order. The reading is post-biblical Christian exegesis; the structural fact it leans on — that Abram tithed to a non-Aaronic priest before the Aaronic priesthood existed — is direct. // **Later prophetic tithe-material.** **Mal 3:8-10** — *will a man rob Elohim? Yet you rob me. But you say, How have we robbed you? In tithes and offerings... bring the whole tithe into the storehouse* — the post-exilic prophetic indictment of withheld tithes. The Malachi material extends the tithe-institution into the Second Temple liturgical-economic frame; it is the canonical Hebrew Bible's last major tithe-passage. // **The WoH translation renders 'a tithe of all'**, matching ASV / NRSV / NJPS consensus. The first-occurrence status, the pre-Sinai / pre-Aaronic locus, the Gen 28:22 Jacob-tithe cross-reference, the ANE tithe-context, and the Hebrews 7 downstream reception are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:20. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the first-occurrence status is verifiable by lexical search of the Hebrew Bible; (b) the pre-Sinai locus is grammatically explicit (the verse is at Gen 14, before Exod 19's Sinai event); (c) the Lev 27 / Num 18 / Deut 12-14 / Deut 26 cross-references for the developed tithe-legislation are grammatically explicit; (d) the Gen 28:22 Jacob-tithe parallel is grammatically explicit; (e) the Hebrews 7:1-10 downstream reception is flagged as post-biblical without entering the translation.

**Gen 14:22 is the chapter's keystone theological move: Abram's oath identifies YHWH appositionally with El Elyon.** *Harimoti yadi el-YHWH El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz* — *I have lifted my hand to YHWH, El Elyon, possessor of heaven and earth*. The construction places **YHWH** (the Abrahamic divine name) in apposition with **El Elyon** (the Melchizedek-tradition divine name from vv 18-20). The appositional reading is the natural Hebrew syntax — no waw conjunctive between the names, no construct relation — and yields: *YHWH [who is] El Elyon, possessor of heaven and earth*. The Genesis editor's narrative move is to harmonize the two divine-name traditions by identifying them. // **The appositional construction is the consensus reading.** **LXX** renders *kyrion ton theon ton hypsiston* — *the Lord, the Most High God* — preserving the apposition. **Samaritan Pentateuch** preserves the appositional reading. **Targum Onkelos** reads *Memra de-YHWH El Elyon* (the Memra of YHWH El Elyon), preserving the appositional identification. **Vulgate** reads *ad Dominum Deum excelsum* — same apposition. The reading is uncontested across the early versions. // **The textual variant.** A small minority of textual witnesses preserves a different reading: a fragment of the **Samaritan tradition** and some **Peshitta witnesses** omit *YHWH* and read only *El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz*. The variant has been read by **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 208) and others as preserving the *older* form of the verse, with the *YHWH* gloss being a later Yahwistic harmonization. The reasoning: at the pre-redactional level, Abram swears by *El Elyon* (matching Melchizedek's blessing-formula at vv 19-20); a later Yahwistic editor adds *YHWH* in apposition to identify the patriarchal divine name with the canonical Israelite divine name. The reading is plausible source-critical analysis and is consistent with the broader patriarchal-El-religion reconstruction (Alt 1929, Cross 1973). The MT reading with *YHWH* is what the canonical text preserves; the variant is a redactional shadow. **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, p. 51) discusses the variant in the context of the El-YHWH harmonization-pattern. // **The harmonization-pattern across Genesis.** The Gen 14:22 move is one instance of a broader Genesis editorial pattern: identifying the patriarchal-stratum *El*-religion with the *YHWH*-name. **Exod 6:2-3** is the most explicit harmonization-passage: *Va-y'daber Elohim el-Moshe va-yomer elav ani YHWH; va-era el-Avraham el-Yitzchak v-el-Ya'akov b-El Shaddai u-shemi YHWH lo nodati lahem* — *And Elohim spoke to Moses and said to him, I am YHWH. I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them*. The Exod 6:3 verse explicitly states the harmonization: the patriarchal-stratum divine name was *El Shaddai*, the Mosaic-stratum revelation is *YHWH*, and the two are the *same deity under different names*. The Gen 14:22 appositional construction is the Genesis-narrative-stratum version of the same harmonization-move, applied to the *El Elyon* of the Melchizedek tradition. The pattern recurs with *El Olam* (Gen 21:33: *va-yiqra sham b-shem YHWH El Olam* — *and he called there on the name of YHWH, El Olam* — the same appositional construction at the Beer-Sheba sanctuary); *El Bethel* (Gen 31:13, 35:7); *El Elohei Yisra'el* (Gen 33:20). Each patriarchal sanctuary-site preserves an *El*-name with a distinctive epithet; the Genesis editor harmonizes each with YHWH. // **The lens-relevant editorial-syncretism.** The Gen 14:22 move is *direct* in that it is grammatically explicit in the text: Abram swears by *YHWH El Elyon*, with no conjunction between the names, in apposition. The Genesis editor explicitly is identifying YHWH with the El Elyon of the Melchizedek tradition. The fact that this is an editorial move (rather than a pre-textual fact) is the modern critical reading — that the harmonization is a Genesis-editor's harmonization-of-traditions move, working to unify the El-stratum patriarchal religion with the YHWH-stratum Mosaic religion. **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973), **Smith** (*The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001), **Albertz** (*A History of Israelite Religion in the Old Testament Period*, 1994), and **Mark Smith** (*The Early History of God*, 2nd ed. 2002) treat the pattern in detail. // **The WoH translation renders 'YHWH, El Elyon, possessor of heaven and earth'** with comma-apposition, matching ASV (which reads 'Jehovah, God Most High'). The comma-apposition preserves the Hebrew construction (no conjunctive between the names) while making the appositional reading unambiguous in English. The harmonization-reading, the textual variant, and the broader Genesis El-YHWH editorial-pattern are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:22. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the appositional construction is grammatically explicit at Gen 14:22; (b) the LXX, Samaritan, Targum, and Vulgate uniform appositional reading is direct documentary evidence; (c) the broader Genesis harmonization-pattern (*El Shaddai* at Exod 6:3, *El Olam* at Gen 21:33, *El Bethel* at Gen 31:13 / 35:7, *El Elohei Yisra'el* at Gen 33:20) is grammatically explicit at each cited verse; (d) the textual variant (omission of *YHWH*) is direct manuscript evidence even though minority. The redactional reading is scholarly synthesis (Westermann, Cross), but the synthesis is the mainstream modern critical consensus on the Genesis editor's harmonization-of-traditions work.
